kp-306.title: KP1: Energy as an indirect concept

questions.prompt: Why do scientists often study energy indirectly?
choices: Energy is identified mainly by naming the material that contains it.
Energy can be measured best by ignoring changes while the system is active.
Energy cannot usually be seen directly, so scientists observe the changes it causes.
Energy is easiest to confirm when a system gives off light.
questions.explanation: The textbook presents energy as something investigated through effects such as motion, temperature change, sound, light, or position changes.

questions.prompt: A dropper popper launches upward after being inverted and released. What is the best evidence that energy was involved?
choices: The popper's shape changed before release but its motion did not change afterward.
The system changed motion and position.
The system was named before the trial began.
The popper returned to its original colour after the trial.
questions.explanation: A visible change in motion or position is indirect evidence that energy has affected the system.

questions.prompt: Which observation would be the weakest evidence for energy in a system?
choices: A ball speeds up as it rolls downhill.
A lamp gives off light and warms nearby air.
A stretched elastic band snaps back.
A stationary object is described without any observed change.
questions.explanation: A name label does not show a change caused by energy, while motion, light, thermal change, and elastic action do.

questions.prompt: Which statement best connects energy and change in a system?
choices: Energy is useful for explaining a system after the changes are ignored.
Energy can cause changes such as motion, heating, sound, light, or shape change.
Energy is the same thing as the material that makes up a system.
Energy matters mainly when matter leaves the system boundary.
questions.explanation: The chapter frames energy through the changes it produces in a defined system.

questions.prompt: A bungee jumper and cord move from a greater height to a lower height. Which change helps show energy is involved?
choices: The bridge remains attached to the ground.
The cord is part of the scene being studied.
The system changes position and motion.
The surroundings include objects outside the system.
questions.explanation: A change in height and motion is a useful observation for analyzing energy.

questions.prompt: Which student statement is most scientifically useful?
choices: Energy is present when I can identify a change and connect it to the system.
Energy is present when the system has a label naming an energy form.
Energy is present when matter leaves the system boundary.
Energy is present when it is visible as a substance.
questions.explanation: Energy is not usually observed as a substance; its effects are observed through changes.

questions.prompt: In an investigation of a skateboarder rolling down a ramp, what could be a useful system?
choices: The air and ramp, while excluding the moving skateboarder.
The skateboarder and skateboard being studied.
Everything outside the skateboarder and skateboard.
The measuring tape because it defines the system boundary.
questions.explanation: A system is the object, group, or process chosen for observation.

questions.prompt: Why must a scientist define the system before analyzing energy?
choices: It clarifies what is being studied and what counts as surroundings.
It makes the chosen objects stop interacting with their surroundings.
It removes friction and air resistance from the investigation.
It turns the chosen boundary into a barrier that blocks energy transfer.
questions.explanation: Defining the system sets the boundary for tracking energy changes and exchanges.

questions.prompt: Two students study the same bungee jump. One defines the system as the jumper and cord; another includes the bridge. Which statement is correct?
choices: The larger system is automatically more scientific than the smaller one.
The system should include the bridge because the bridge is the largest object.
Both definitions can be valid if each student states the boundary clearly.
The surroundings are not needed if the system has more than one object.
questions.explanation: The textbook emphasizes that people define systems to suit the question being studied.

questions.prompt: If the system is a person and a bungee cord, what are the surroundings?
choices: The bridge, air, Earth, and anything else outside the chosen system.
The person alone, because motion is easiest to see there.
The cord alone, because it stretches and stores energy.
The parts of the system that are moving fastest.
questions.explanation: The surroundings are everything outside the defined system.

questions.prompt: Which statement best matches the idea universe = system + surroundings?
choices: The surroundings are chosen first, and the system is whatever remains.
The system and surroundings together account for everything being considered.
The surroundings include mainly objects that touch the system.
The universe excludes the system once the boundary is drawn.
questions.explanation: The relationship helps separate what is under observation from everything outside it.

questions.prompt: Air resistance slows a falling jumper. In a jumper-and-cord system, where does some mechanical energy go?
choices: It is stored as extra mass in the bungee cord.
It becomes chemical potential energy in the bridge.
It is removed from the energy analysis because the jumper slows down.
It is transferred or transformed into the surroundings, including air.
questions.explanation: Interactions with surroundings can transfer or transform energy; energy is not destroyed.

questions.prompt: Which list best summarizes key properties of energy from Chapter 3.1?
choices: Energy causes change, has different forms, can transfer or transform, and depends on physical quantities.
Energy has many names, but it does not transfer between objects.
Energy is created when a system boundary is drawn.
Energy is useful mainly when little of it becomes thermal energy.
questions.explanation: These are the organizing properties introduced at the start of Chapter 3.1.

questions.prompt: Which observation uses more than one property of energy?
choices: A bulb connected to a battery gives light while also warming slightly.
A notebook has pages that can be counted.
A desk has a rectangular top and four legs.
A pencil has graphite inside a wooden case.
questions.explanation: The bulb example involves energy forms, transformation, transfer, and useful plus less useful outputs.

questions.prompt: Why are physical quantities important when studying energy?
choices: Quantities replace observations because energy is not connected to change.
Quantities such as mass, speed, height, and gravity can affect the amount of certain forms of energy.
Quantities prove that energy is a visible substance.
Quantities matter mainly after energy has stopped transferring.
questions.explanation: Chapter 3.1 later connects mass, velocity, gravitational field strength, and height to mechanical energy.

questions.prompt: Which system clearly has kinetic energy?
choices: A stretched elastic band held motionless.
A cyclist moving down a trail.
A battery not connected to a circuit.
A book sitting still on a shelf.
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion.

questions.prompt: Which statement shows that kinetic energy can occur at different scales?
choices: It can involve moving bicycles, moving particles, moving electrons, or vibrating matter.
It is limited to large visible objects moving from one place to another.
It is stored when an object is above a reference point.
It is the same as chemical potential energy in bonds.
questions.explanation: The textbook connects kinetic energy to motion of objects, particles, electrons, and vibrations.

questions.prompt: Which example is least likely to be classified as kinetic energy?
choices: Moving electrons in a wire.
Particles vibrating in warm water.
A sound wave travelling through air.
Energy stored in a compressed spring.
questions.explanation: A compressed spring stores elastic potential energy; the other examples involve motion.

questions.prompt: Which example best shows potential energy?
choices: A ball rolling quickly across the floor.
A stretched bowstring before release.
Sound travelling through air.
Electrons moving through a wire.
questions.explanation: Potential energy is stored energy due to position or condition; a stretched bowstring stores elastic potential energy.

questions.prompt: A mountain biker pauses at the top of a hill. Which stored energy is most directly connected to position?
choices: Mechanical kinetic energy.
Electrical kinetic energy.
Gravitational potential energy.
Sound energy.
questions.explanation: Height above a reference point gives the biker and bike gravitational potential energy.

questions.prompt: Which statement is a common misconception about potential energy?
choices: Potential energy means nothing is stored because the object is not moving.
Potential energy can be stored because of an object's position.
Potential energy can be stored in chemical bonds.
Potential energy can be stored in stretched or compressed objects.
questions.explanation: An object can store potential energy even when it is not moving.

questions.prompt: Which example has mechanical kinetic energy?
choices: Particles moving randomly in hot soup.
A soccer ball rolling across a field.
Electrons flowing through a wire.
A chemical fuel stored in a tank.
questions.explanation: Mechanical kinetic energy is motion energy of objects larger than atoms and molecules.

questions.prompt: Why is a moving car classified differently from moving electrons in a wire?
choices: The car has no kinetic energy because it is much larger than atoms.
Moving electrons are classified as thermal energy because they are small.
The car has mechanical kinetic energy, while moving electrons have electrical kinetic energy.
Both are chemical potential energy because both can do work.
questions.explanation: The type of kinetic energy depends on what is moving.

questions.prompt: Which observation would most directly show an increase in mechanical kinetic energy?
choices: A stopped cart is painted a new colour.
A stationary spring is compressed and held.
A cart of the same mass moves faster down a ramp.
A battery is connected to a circuit.
questions.explanation: For the same object, greater speed means greater mechanical kinetic energy.

questions.prompt: What is thermal energy?
choices: The energy of visible light travelling through space.
The energy of random particle motion in a substance.
The energy stored in an object's position above the ground.
The energy of electrons moving through a conductor.
questions.explanation: Thermal energy is related to the random motion of particles in matter.

questions.prompt: In scientific language, what is heat?
choices: Any thermal energy contained inside an object at one moment.
Thermal energy transferred from one object to another.
Energy stored in chemical bonds.
Light energy travelling from the Sun.
questions.explanation: The textbook distinguishes thermal energy from heat: heat is thermal energy in transfer.

questions.prompt: A warm metal spoon is placed in cooler water. Which statement uses the terms correctly?
choices: Heat transfers from the spoon to the water because of the temperature difference.
Thermal energy transfers from the water to the spoon because water is a liquid.
Cold transfers into the spoon until both objects have no thermal energy.
The spoon stores heat permanently because it started warmer.
questions.explanation: Thermal energy transfers from warmer matter to cooler matter; that transferred thermal energy is heat.

kp-315.title: KP5: Radiant, electrical kinetic, and sound energy

questions.prompt: Which example is radiant energy?
choices: A marble rolling across a table.
Visible light from a lamp travelling as electromagnetic waves.
A spring held in a compressed position.
A battery stored in a drawer.
questions.explanation: Radiant energy is energy carried by electromagnetic waves, including visible light and infrared radiation.

questions.prompt: What is electrical kinetic energy?
choices: Energy stored by separated charges in a battery before a circuit is complete.
Energy of moving electrons in a conductor.
Energy of an object because it is above a reference point.
Energy stored in stretched or compressed materials.
questions.explanation: Electrical kinetic energy involves moving electrons, such as current in a wire.

questions.prompt: Why is sound energy classified as kinetic energy?
choices: It is stored in chemical bonds until an object makes noise.
It is a type of radiant energy because it can be heard at a distance.
It involves vibrations and disturbances of particles in matter.
It is gravitational potential energy when the sound source is above the listener.
questions.explanation: Sound travels as vibrating particles pass a pressure disturbance through matter.

questions.prompt: Which pairing correctly matches a stored energy example to a form of potential energy?
choices: A moving skateboard - elastic potential energy.
A stretched tennis racquet string - elastic potential energy.
A ringing bell - chemical potential energy.
Moving electrons in a circuit - electrical potential energy.
questions.explanation: Elastic potential energy is stored in stretched or compressed objects.

questions.prompt: Which form of potential energy is stored in separated positive and negative charges, such as in a cell or battery?
choices: Thermal energy, because the cell may warm during use.
Electrical kinetic energy, because charges can later move in a circuit.
Electrical potential energy, because separated charges can do work when connected.
Radiant energy, because batteries are often used to power lamps.
questions.explanation: Electrical potential energy is stored because charges are separated.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly describes nuclear energy in Chapter 3.1?
choices: It is the vibration pattern that carries sound through air.
It is the motion of electrons through a conductor.
It is the random particle motion measured as temperature.
It is energy stored within atomic nuclei.
questions.explanation: Nuclear energy is a form of potential energy stored in the nucleus of an atom.

questions.prompt: A mountain biker is moving along a trail high above the valley floor. Which statement is best?
choices: The biker has gravitational potential energy, while motion energy is not relevant.
The biker has mechanical kinetic energy, while height does not affect the system.
The biker has both mechanical kinetic energy and gravitational potential energy.
The biker has mainly sound energy because the bicycle makes noise.
questions.explanation: A moving object above a reference point can have both kinetic and potential energy.

questions.prompt: When does a coaster car likely have both gravitational potential energy and mechanical kinetic energy?
choices: When it is parked at the bottom of the track and not moving.
When it is moving on level ground chosen as the reference point.
When it is lifted at the top and held completely at rest.
While moving partway down a hill above the reference point.
questions.explanation: At an intermediate height with motion, the car has height-related potential energy and motion-related kinetic energy.

questions.prompt: Which question best helps identify multiple forms of energy in a system?
choices: Does the object have a clear label for the energy form?
Is anything moving, and is anything stored because of position or condition?
Is the system easy to describe without measuring change?
Does the object have a colour associated with energy?
questions.explanation: Motion points to kinetic energy, while position or condition can indicate potential energy.

kp-318.title: KP1: Law of conservation of energy

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes the law of conservation of energy?
choices: Energy is counted after useful outputs, but less useful outputs are left out.
Energy is made by devices when they begin doing a useful task.
Energy is not created or destroyed; it is transferred or transformed.
Energy conservation applies mainly when motion is easy to see.
questions.explanation: The total energy before a transfer or transformation equals the total energy afterward.

questions.prompt: A lamp changes electrical energy into light and thermal energy. How does conservation of energy apply?
choices: The thermal energy is ignored because the lamp was designed to make light.
The total energy is accounted for in useful light plus less useful thermal energy.
Electrical energy becomes conserved after the bulb finishes warming up.
The useful light energy should be greater than the input energy.
questions.explanation: Even less useful outputs are still part of the total energy after transformation.

questions.prompt: A student says, "The sled lost energy because it did not reach the same height." What is the best correction?
choices: The sled's mass decreased enough to lower its gravitational potential energy.
The reference point moved, so the original height no longer matters.
The snow stored the sled's speed as chemical potential energy.
Some mechanical energy transformed into thermal and sound energy, so total energy was still conserved.
questions.explanation: Apparent energy loss usually means energy has become less useful or moved to surroundings, not that it was destroyed.

questions.prompt: What is an energy transformation?
choices: Energy moving between objects while keeping the same form.
Energy changing from one form to another.
Matter crossing the boundary of an open system.
A system boundary being redrawn for a new investigation.
questions.explanation: Transformation means the form of energy changes.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is an energy transformation in a battery-powered flashlight?
choices: Radiant energy to electrical potential energy to sound energy.
Mechanical kinetic energy to gravitational potential energy inside the battery.
Chemical potential energy to electrical energy to radiant and thermal energy.
Thermal energy to chemical potential energy without any current.
questions.explanation: The textbook's circuit examples connect stored chemical energy to electrical energy and then to light and thermal energy.

questions.prompt: Which example is mainly a transformation rather than only a transfer?
choices: One pool ball gives mechanical kinetic energy to another pool ball.
A warm mug transfers thermal energy to cooler air.
A student slides a marker from one side of a desk to another.
A bulb changes electrical energy into light and thermal energy.
questions.explanation: The bulb changes energy from one form to other forms, so it is a transformation.

questions.prompt: What is energy transfer?
choices: Energy changing from chemical potential energy into radiant energy.
Energy moving from one object or place to another while staying in the same form.
Matter crossing a boundary while energy stays inside the system.
A system becoming easier to draw because the boundary is simpler.
questions.explanation: Transfer means the energy moves between objects or locations without necessarily changing form.

questions.prompt: One pool ball strikes another, and the second ball begins moving. Which energy transfer occurs?
choices: Chemical potential energy transfers from the table surface to the balls.
Sound energy transfers from the first ball into stored elastic energy in the second.
Mechanical kinetic energy transfers from the first ball to the second ball.
Radiant energy transfers from the cue into the second ball.
questions.explanation: The pool example in the textbook shows mechanical kinetic energy moving from one ball to another.

questions.prompt: Which observation shows transfer without requiring a form change?
choices: Electrical energy changes into radiant energy in a bulb.
Chemical potential energy changes into thermal energy in a flame.
Elastic potential energy changes into motion in a catapult.
Thermal energy moves from hot soup to a cooler spoon.
questions.explanation: Thermal energy can transfer from one object to another as thermal energy.

questions.prompt: Why is no energy transformation 100 percent efficient?
choices: Some energy becomes less useful for the intended task, often as thermal or sound energy.
The input energy becomes smaller because conservation stops during friction.
Useful energy appears when a system avoids interacting with its surroundings.
The output form must match the input form for a task to be useful.
questions.explanation: The textbook describes less useful energy outputs while still conserving total energy.

questions.prompt: A light bulb is intended to produce visible light but also warms up. What is the warm bulb evidence of?
choices: The bulb stores the electrical energy as gravitational potential energy.
Some input energy transformed into less useful thermal energy.
The light energy left the system before the circuit was closed.
The bulb changes matter into energy as the filament glows.
questions.explanation: Thermal energy is a common byproduct that may not be useful for the intended task.

questions.prompt: Which phrase is more scientifically careful than saying energy was lost?
choices: Energy changed into mass that can no longer be measured.
Energy was removed from the calculation because it became sound.
Energy was transformed into forms less useful for the task or transferred to surroundings.
Energy became more useful for the task after friction acted on the system.
questions.explanation: Energy can become less useful or leave the chosen system, but it is not destroyed.

questions.prompt: An uncovered pot of boiling water loses steam and receives energy from the stove. What type of system is it?
choices: Closed system, because the pot still has a clear boundary.
Open system, because both matter and energy cross the boundary.
Isolated system, because the stove is outside the pot.
Closed system, because energy enters but matter leaves.
questions.explanation: An open system can exchange both matter and energy with its surroundings.

questions.prompt: A sealed pressure cooker allows energy transfer but prevents matter from leaving as steam. What type of system does it model?
choices: Open system, because the cooker is being heated.
Isolated system, because the lid reduces matter transfer.
Closed system, because energy can transfer while matter is mostly contained.
Surroundings, because the stove is outside the cooker.
questions.explanation: A closed system exchanges energy but not matter with the surroundings.

questions.prompt: Why is a perfectly isolated cooking system unrealistic?
choices: Insulation changes thermal energy into chemical potential energy in the food.
A system with food must be open because food contains matter.
A closed lid makes matter leave faster than energy enters.
Cooking requires energy transfer into the food, but an isolated system exchanges neither energy nor matter.
questions.explanation: A truly isolated system would prevent the energy exchange needed for cooking.

questions.prompt: If a system releases energy, what happens to the surroundings?
choices: The surroundings absorb that energy.
The surroundings become part of the system boundary.
The surroundings must gain matter at the same time.
The surroundings change the released energy into mass.
questions.explanation: Energy exchange is tracked between the system and its surroundings.

questions.prompt: A hot mug cools on a desk. If the mug and liquid are the system, what is happening?
choices: Energy transfers from the cooler surroundings into the warmer system.
Energy transfers from the system to the cooler surroundings.
The boundary prevents energy from interacting with the desk and air.
Matter transfer is required before thermal energy can leave the mug.
questions.explanation: When a warmer system cools, energy is transferred to the surroundings.

questions.prompt: If a cold pack absorbs energy from a student's hand, which statement is correct?
choices: The hand, as part of the surroundings, releases energy to the cold pack system.
The cold pack must release matter before it can absorb energy.
The system boundary blocks energy because the hand is outside it.
The cold pack changes energy into matter as it feels colder.
questions.explanation: When a system absorbs energy, the surroundings release energy.

questions.prompt: In a pool-ball collision, what can happen at the same time?
choices: Mechanical kinetic energy transfers between balls and some energy transforms into sound and thermal energy.
Mechanical kinetic energy changes into chemical potential energy stored in the table.
The collision shows transfer but rules out thermal and sound byproducts.
The second ball gains energy because matter transfers from the first ball.
questions.explanation: The textbook uses pool balls to show transfer and transformation happening together.

questions.prompt: A catapult launches a small object and makes a snapping sound. Which analysis is best?
choices: Mechanical kinetic energy changes into elastic potential energy after the object leaves.
The sound shows that mechanical kinetic energy was not involved in the launch.
Elastic potential energy transforms into mechanical kinetic energy, and some energy also becomes sound and thermal energy.
The catapult works as an isolated system because it uses stored energy.
questions.explanation: Real devices often involve multiple transformations and transfers, including less useful byproducts.

questions.prompt: Which question helps separate transfer from transformation?
choices: Did the system become open, and did matter cross the boundary?
Did the energy move to another object, and did its form change?
Did the object have more mass after the event?
Did the surroundings contain the largest object in the scene?
questions.explanation: Transfer is about where energy moves; transformation is about whether the form changes.

questions.prompt: Which physical quantities are most directly connected to mechanical kinetic energy?
choices: Height and gravitational field strength.
Mass and velocity.
Temperature and surface colour.
Volume and chemical formula.
questions.explanation: Chapter 3.1 connects mechanical kinetic energy to mass and velocity.

questions.prompt: Which physical quantities affect gravitational potential energy near Earth?
choices: Velocity, direction of motion, and surface texture.
Temperature, sound level, and colour.
Mass, gravitational field strength, and change in height.
Chemical formula, volume, and brightness.
questions.explanation: Gravitational potential energy depends on mass, gravity, and height relative to a reference position.

questions.prompt: Why is the bowling-ball versus table-tennis-ball thought experiment useful?
choices: It compares objects with different mass so the effect on mechanical kinetic energy can be reasoned out.
It compares two heights so gravitational potential energy can be calculated directly.
It shows that velocity is the quantity used for gravitational potential energy.
It treats colour and shape as the main variables in a collision.
questions.explanation: The thought experiment uses everyday experience to identify variables that affect energy.

questions.prompt: Two balls roll at the same velocity. One has twice the mass of the other. Which ball has more mechanical kinetic energy?
choices: The ball with twice the mass.
The lighter ball, because it is easier to start moving.
They have the same mechanical kinetic energy because velocity matches.
The ball with the larger volume, regardless of its mass.
questions.explanation: Mechanical kinetic energy increases when mass increases, if velocity stays the same.

questions.prompt: Two identical carts roll toward a target. One rolls faster. What should you predict?
choices: The slower cart has more mechanical kinetic energy because it stays in contact longer.
Both carts have the same mechanical kinetic energy because their masses match.
The faster cart has more mechanical kinetic energy.
The cart with less sound has more mechanical kinetic energy.
questions.explanation: Velocity affects mechanical kinetic energy; faster motion means more kinetic energy for the same mass.

questions.prompt: Which design choice would most increase a cart's mechanical kinetic energy at the bottom of a ramp?
choices: Increase the cart's speed while keeping mass the same.
Use a reference height that is farther from the ramp.
Choose a smoother colour for the cart body.
Measure the cart after it has stopped at the bottom.
questions.explanation: Speed is a major variable in mechanical kinetic energy.

questions.prompt: Which equation gives mechanical kinetic energy?
choices: Eg = mg delta h
Ek = mv
Ek = 1/2mv^2
Eg = 1/2gv^2
questions.explanation: Mechanical kinetic energy is calculated using Ek = 1/2mv^2.

questions.prompt: A 4.0 kg cart moves at 3.0 m/s. What is its mechanical kinetic energy?
choices: 6 J

questions.prompt: If an object's velocity doubles while its mass stays the same, what happens to its mechanical kinetic energy?
choices: It becomes 2 times as large.
It stays the same because mass did not change.
It becomes half as large because velocity is shared across the motion.
It becomes 4 times as large.
questions.explanation: Velocity is squared in Ek = 1/2mv^2, so doubling velocity multiplies Ek by 4.

questions.prompt: Which variables affect gravitational potential energy?
choices: Velocity, direction of motion, and contact surface.
Mass, gravitational field strength, and change in height.
Mass, velocity, and sound level.
Height, colour, and shape.
questions.explanation: The textbook connects gravitational potential energy to m, g, and change in height.

questions.prompt: Two identical rocks are held above the same ground reference point. One is higher. Which rock has more gravitational potential energy?
choices: The lower rock.
The rock with greater speed before being released.
The higher rock.
The rock that would make less sound when dropped.
questions.explanation: For the same mass and g, greater height means greater gravitational potential energy.

questions.prompt: A ball is lifted 1 m above Earth and 1 m above the Moon. Why is the gravitational potential energy different?
choices: The ball's mass is lower when it is above the Moon.
The reference height becomes zero above the Moon.
The gravitational field strength is different.
The ball changes from potential energy into sound energy.
questions.explanation: The value of g is part of the gravitational potential energy relationship.

questions.prompt: Which equation gives gravitational potential energy near Earth?
choices: Ek = 1/2mv^2
Eg = mg delta h
Eg = 1/2mv^2
Eg = m divided by delta h
questions.explanation: Gravitational potential energy is calculated with Eg = mg delta h.

questions.prompt: A 2.0 kg object is lifted 5.0 m above a reference point on Earth. Using g = 9.8 m/s^2, what is its Eg?
questions.explanation: Eg = mg delta h = 2.0 x 9.8 x 5.0 = 98 J.

questions.prompt: If mass and g stay the same but the change in height doubles, what happens to Eg?
choices: It stays the same because mass and g did not change.
It becomes four times as large.
It becomes half as large.
It doubles.
questions.explanation: Eg is directly proportional to change in height in Eg = mg delta h.

questions.prompt: Why can the same object have different gravitational potential energy values?
choices: The object's mass changes when a lower reference point is chosen.
The value of g changes when a student redraws the diagram.
Gravitational potential energy is measured relative to a chosen reference position.
Mechanical kinetic energy is included in the height measurement.
questions.explanation: The height change depends on the reference position selected for the analysis.

questions.prompt: A sled is at the bottom of a hill. If the bottom is chosen as the reference position, what is its gravitational potential energy there?
choices: Maximum because the sled is closest to the ground.
Zero relative to that reference position.
The same as its mechanical kinetic energy.
9.8 J for each kilogram of sled mass.
questions.explanation: At the chosen reference position, delta h is zero, so Eg is zero relative to that position.

questions.prompt: Which choice would be a practical reference position for analyzing a skier descending a hill?
choices: The skier's speed halfway down the hill.
The sound made by the skis during a turn.
The bottom of the hill.
The mass of the skier and equipment.
questions.explanation: A useful reference position is often the surface or level toward which the object can fall.

kp-331.title: KP7: Friction and apparent energy loss

questions.prompt: Why does a sled usually not reach the same height on the next hill?
choices: The sled's mass decreases as it slides over snow.
Friction transforms some mechanical energy into thermal and sound energy.
The reference position rises with the sled as it moves.
Gravitational potential energy changes into chemical energy in the air.
questions.explanation: Friction makes some energy less useful for climbing by transforming it into thermal and sound energy.

questions.prompt: What happens at the contact surfaces as a sled slides over snow?
choices: The sled gains gravitational potential energy at the contact surface.
Attractive forces between surface atoms are continually broken and reformed, producing thermal and sound energy.
The snow changes the sled's mass into mechanical kinetic energy.
The reference point absorbs the sled's remaining height.
questions.explanation: The textbook links friction to interactions at surfaces that produce thermal and sound energy.

questions.prompt: Which statement is most consistent with conservation of energy?
choices: The sled has less useful mechanical energy, but total energy is still accounted for.
The sled's energy is missing because it became too spread out to measure.
Friction means conservation applies before the sled starts moving.
The sled's gravitational potential energy becomes mass in the snow.
questions.explanation: Energy is conserved even when some becomes less useful or transfers to surroundings.

questions.prompt: What is the energy idea behind a Rube Goldberg machine?
choices: A sequence of energy transfers and transformations completes a simple task.
A machine performs a task by keeping energy in the same object.
A task is completed by avoiding collisions between parts.
A machine works when its energy pathway is hidden from observation.
questions.explanation: The investigation describes a machine that uses many transfers and transformations to perform a task.

questions.prompt: A marble rolls down a ramp, hits a lever, and the lever drops a ball into a cup. What should be identified?
choices: The final task without describing how earlier parts caused it.
Each transfer or transformation that triggers the next step.
The number of materials, because material count is the energy pathway.
The fastest part, while leaving slower parts outside the system.
questions.explanation: A chain of energy changes explains how one event leads to the next.

questions.prompt: Which observation best shows a chain of energy changes?
choices: A sketch lists materials before testing begins.
A machine sits ready with stored elastic energy but is not released.
One moving part causes another part to move, which triggers a later step.
A final task happens after the starting step is removed.
questions.explanation: A chain requires connected events where energy changes or moves from one part to another.

questions.prompt: A Rube Goldberg machine works only sometimes. Which design change most directly improves energy transfer reliability?
choices: Add rougher surfaces where the marble needs to keep rolling.
Align the ramp and lever so the marble hits the same spot each trial.
Increase the number of steps without testing their timing.
Place the trigger farther away from the part it must move.
questions.explanation: Design affects whether energy is transferred reliably from one part to the next.

questions.prompt: Which design change most likely reduces unwanted energy loss?
choices: Add rough tape to sliding surfaces so motion slows more predictably.
Make moving parts collide off-centre to spread energy through the frame.
Use smoother contact surfaces where sliding causes too much friction.
Let supports wobble so the machine can adjust during each trial.
questions.explanation: Reducing friction can keep more mechanical energy available for the intended task.

questions.prompt: Why is testing and modifying part of the Rube Goldberg investigation?
choices: A sketch can show the intended task, but trials reveal whether energy changes happen reliably.
Testing makes the machine isolated from its surroundings.
Modifying a machine changes the law of conservation for that design.
Trials are mainly for making the machine look more complicated.
questions.explanation: Testing helps evaluate and improve the design's energy pathway.

questions.prompt: Which observation is useful evidence of energy transfer or transformation in a Rube Goldberg machine?
choices: A collision, a sound, a height change, or a moving part.
The list of materials before the machine is released.
The colour pattern chosen for the machine parts.
The number of students observing the trial.
questions.explanation: Visible and audible changes can provide evidence for tracing energy.

questions.prompt: A ball hits a cup, the cup dents slightly, and a sound is heard. What should the student record?
choices: The cup's brand, because material labels identify energy form.
The cup's colour before the trial, because colour is the main energy evidence.
Collision, deformation, motion, and sound as evidence of energy changes.
The final location of the ball without describing the impact.
questions.explanation: Motion, sound, and deformation are all clues for explaining energy transfer or transformation.

questions.prompt: Which statement avoids overclaiming from evidence?
choices: A sound supports an energy explanation, but the system and surroundings should be defined.
A collision means the energy pathway is complete even without later observations.
A height change shows matter crossed the boundary of the system.
A dent shows the energy form did not change during the trial.
questions.explanation: Observations should be connected carefully to a defined system and the energy pathway.

questions.prompt: In Investigation 3-B, what kind of evidence would help describe an energy transformation?
choices: The name of the procedure without observations from the test.
A change in form, motion, height, temperature, light, or sound in the system.
The size of the classroom where the trial took place.
The date written above the data table.
questions.explanation: The investigation asks students to use observations to describe energy transfer or transformation.

questions.prompt: A falling container hits sand and leaves a deeper mark when dropped from higher up. Which energy idea is being investigated?
choices: The sand creates mechanical kinetic energy at the moment of impact.
Stored gravitational potential energy transforms into mechanical kinetic energy and then affects the sand.
The container's energy depends mainly on the colour of the lid.
The impact mark forms because gravitational field strength becomes zero.
questions.explanation: The motion and impact help reveal transformations from height-related stored energy to motion and other effects.

questions.prompt: Why should students choose variables and keep other factors constant in an energy investigation?
choices: So changes in observations can be linked more clearly to the variable being tested.
So the system becomes isolated from energy transfer during the test.
So the data table can be replaced with a description of materials.
So the dependent variable changes before the independent variable is chosen.
questions.explanation: A controlled experiment helps connect observed energy changes to the factor under investigation.

questions.prompt: In a ramp investigation, a rolling marble hits a stationary toy car and the car begins to move. Which statement best describes the energy transfer?
choices: Mechanical kinetic energy changes into gravitational potential energy in the toy car.
The toy car gains motion because matter transfers from the marble.
Thermal energy moves from the ramp into the toy car as the main transfer.
Mechanical kinetic energy moves from the marble to the toy car during the collision.
questions.explanation: The collision transfers mechanical kinetic energy from the moving marble to the toy car, causing the car to move.

questions.prompt: A group is testing how energy moves in a system made of a ball, a ramp, and a small cup. Which observation would best help them track energy transfer?
choices: The release height chosen before the ball starts moving.
Whether the ball changes the cup's motion, position, or shape after contact.
The total number of parts included in the setup.
The material list written before the first trial.
questions.explanation: Changes in the cup's motion, position, or shape are evidence that energy was transferred from another part of the system.

questions.prompt: A warm container is placed in a cooler foam box with ice. Which description correctly tracks energy transfer between the system and surroundings?
choices: Thermal energy transfers from the warmer container toward the colder ice and air inside the box.
The ice transfers cold energy into the container as a separate energy form.
Energy transfer requires steam or other matter to leave the box.
Mechanical kinetic energy transfers from the container to the ice.
questions.explanation: Thermal energy transfers from warmer objects to cooler surroundings; observing temperature changes helps track that transfer.

questions.prompt: A student asks whether release height affects how far a toy car rolls after leaving a ramp. What is the independent variable?
choices: The distance the toy car rolls after leaving the ramp.
The release height of the toy car.
The same toy car kept for each trial.
The energy transformed after the car leaves the ramp.
questions.explanation: The independent variable is the factor deliberately changed: the toy car's release height.

questions.prompt: A class tests whether a falling object's mass affects the impact it makes in sand. Which factor should be kept constant to make the test fair?
choices: The mass of the falling object.
The result recorded after impact.
The depth of the mark made in the sand.
The drop height.
questions.explanation: If mass is being tested, drop height should stay constant so changes in impact can be linked more clearly to mass.

questions.prompt: A model coaster reaches the end of the track only half the time. Which design change most directly targets unwanted energy loss?
choices: Use a rougher track surface so the car vibrates more.
Add unnecessary bends that make the car scrape the sides.
Make the wheels and track smoother and better aligned.
Lower the starting point while keeping the same track.
questions.explanation: Smoother, better-aligned contact can reduce friction, so more mechanical energy remains useful for motion along the track.

questions.prompt: A coaster car starts from rest at the top of a hill and rolls downward. Which energy change is the main one as it descends?
choices: Mechanical kinetic energy transforms into gravitational potential energy.
Gravitational potential energy transforms into mechanical kinetic energy.
Thermal energy transforms into chemical potential energy.
Sound energy transforms into mass.
questions.explanation: As height decreases, gravitational potential energy decreases while mechanical kinetic energy increases.

questions.prompt: Assume a model coaster has 120 J of total mechanical energy and friction is ignored. At one point its gravitational potential energy is 45 J. What is its mechanical kinetic energy? Use E_total = E_g + E_k.
questions.explanation: With no friction, E_k = 120 J - 45 J = 75 J.

questions.prompt: Which statement best compares the top, middle, and bottom of a frictionless coaster hill?
choices: The top has the most mechanical kinetic energy, and the bottom has the most gravitational potential energy.
The three positions have the same total mechanical energy, so each energy form has the same value.
The top has more gravitational potential energy, the bottom has more mechanical kinetic energy, and the middle has a mixture of both.
The middle has less total energy because it is between the top and bottom.
questions.explanation: A coaster trades gravitational potential energy and mechanical kinetic energy as its height and speed change.

questions.prompt: A frictionless coaster travels through points at heights of 70 m, 40 m, 20 m, and 5 m. At which point should its speed be greatest?
choices: 5 m
questions.explanation: The lowest point has the least gravitational potential energy and the greatest mechanical kinetic energy, so speed is greatest there.

questions.prompt: A cart's gravitational potential energy decreases by 300 J as it rolls down a track with negligible friction. How much does its mechanical kinetic energy increase?
choices: 0 J
questions.explanation: If friction is negligible, the lost gravitational potential energy becomes mechanical kinetic energy, so E_k increases by 300 J.

questions.prompt: Which prediction correctly uses the relationship between height and speed on a coaster track?
choices: The car is fastest at the highest point because height gives it the most speed.
The car is fastest after it has moved to a lower point, where more energy is in motion.
The car's speed depends on height mainly when the track is nearly flat.
The car slows at a low point because gravitational potential energy is lower there.
questions.explanation: Moving to a lower height can transform gravitational potential energy into mechanical kinetic energy, increasing speed.

questions.prompt: A real coaster climbs a second hill that is lower than its starting hill, even when no motor pushes it. What is the best explanation?
choices: Some mechanical energy has transformed into thermal energy and sound because of friction.
The coaster's mass increases as it moves, reducing the height it can reach.
The second hill has a different reference point, so energy conservation does not apply.
Gravitational potential energy changes into chemical potential energy in the track.
questions.explanation: Friction and vibrations transform some mechanical energy into less useful forms such as thermal energy and sound, so less remains for climbing.

questions.prompt: A frictionless calculation predicts a coaster speed of 18 m/s at the bottom of a hill. Which result is most realistic for an actual coaster on the same track?
choices: 18 m/s or slightly higher, because friction adds energy to the coaster.
Less than 18 m/s, because some energy is transformed into thermal and sound energy.
The same speed at each height, because total energy is conserved.
A speed based mainly on the coaster's colour and shape.
questions.explanation: Real friction reduces the mechanical kinetic energy available for speed, although total energy is still conserved.

questions.prompt: Which design choice would most likely help a model coaster keep more useful mechanical energy?
choices: Use rough joints that make the wheels bounce and scrape.
Make the car rub against the side supports on sharper turns.
Use smoother wheel contact and a track shape that avoids sharp, scraping turns.
Cover the track with a sticky surface so the car slows quickly.
questions.explanation: Reducing friction and scraping helps less mechanical energy transform into thermal and sound energy.

questions.prompt: Which example best shows chemical potential energy being transformed into another form?
choices: A food molecule stored before a reaction begins.
A battery-powered flashlight producing light and thermal energy.
A glass mirror redirecting incoming light.
A book raised higher above the floor.
questions.explanation: Chemical potential energy stored in the battery is transformed into electrical energy, light, and some thermal energy.

questions.prompt: A snack bar helps a cyclist keep moving during a long ride. What stored energy source is the cyclist using?
choices: Nuclear energy stored in the cyclist's helmet.
Chemical potential energy stored in food molecules.
Sound energy stored in the bicycle chain.
Gravitational potential energy stored in the road surface.
questions.explanation: Food molecules store chemical potential energy that cells can transform for movement and other body processes.

questions.prompt: Which misconception should be corrected when describing chemical potential energy?
choices: Chemical potential energy is connected to the arrangement of atoms and bonds.
Chemical reactions can transform stored chemical energy into heat, light, sound, or motion.
Chemical potential energy is a fuel idea, so living cells use a different kind of stored energy.
Fuels and foods can both store chemical potential energy.
questions.explanation: Chemical potential energy is found in many substances, including fuels, foods, and biological molecules.

kp-342.title: KP2: System and surroundings in chemical reactions

questions.prompt: In a reaction between vinegar and baking soda inside a beaker, what is usually treated as the system?
choices: The air outside the beaker.
The reacting vinegar and baking soda mixture.
The lab bench but not the chemicals.
The student observing the reaction.
questions.explanation: For a chemical reaction, the reacting materials are usually defined as the system being studied.

questions.prompt: A hand warmer becomes warmer after its chemicals react. Which direction of energy movement is most likely?
choices: Energy moves from the reacting system to the surroundings.
Energy moves from the surroundings into the reacting system.
Energy becomes chemical potential energy with no transfer to the hand.
Energy movement requires the packet to exchange matter with the air.
questions.explanation: If the surroundings feel warmer, energy has been released by the reacting system to the surroundings.

questions.prompt: A cold pack becomes colder after the inner pouch is broken and the chemicals mix. What does this suggest?
choices: The reaction absorbs energy from the surroundings.
The reaction releases energy to the student's hand.
The surroundings release matter into the system.
The chemicals stop interacting with the surroundings.
questions.explanation: A cooling effect suggests that the reacting system absorbs energy from its surroundings.

kp-343.title: KP3: Bond energy and reaction energy

questions.prompt: Why do different chemical reactions release or absorb different amounts of energy?
choices: Bond energy depends mainly on the colour of the container.
The number of letters in each chemical name controls the energy change.
Products have higher chemical potential energy in any reaction that gives off light.
Different reactants and products have different arrangements of atoms and bonds.
questions.explanation: The energy transformed depends on the chemical potential energy associated with bonds in the reactants and products.

questions.prompt: A reaction has reactants with 920 kJ of chemical potential energy and products with 740 kJ. What is the best conclusion?
choices: The system absorbs 180 kJ from the surroundings.
The system releases 180 kJ to the surroundings.
The system releases 1660 kJ to the surroundings.
No energy is transformed because both values are chemical energy.
questions.explanation: The products are 180 kJ lower in chemical potential energy, so that energy is released by the system.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly compares reactants and products when predicting reaction energy?
choices: The reactants matter more because products no longer store chemical potential energy.
The products matter more because reactants are used up during the reaction.
The difference between reactant and product chemical potential energy matters.
The side with more molecules is the side with lower chemical potential energy.
questions.explanation: Energy release or absorption depends on the relative chemical potential energy of reactants and products.

questions.prompt: A reaction makes the beaker feel warmer. Which classification best fits the reaction?
choices: Endothermic, because energy is released to the surroundings.
Exothermic, because energy is released to the surroundings.
Nuclear, because a warmer beaker means nuclei changed.
Endothermic, because the beaker absorbed matter from the reaction.
questions.explanation: A warmer surrounding beaker is evidence that the system released energy, so the reaction is exothermic.

questions.prompt: In an energy diagram, products are higher in chemical potential energy than reactants. What must have happened?
choices: The reaction absorbed energy from the surroundings.
The reaction released energy to the surroundings.
The reaction converted the extra energy into mass.
The reaction became exothermic after the products formed.
questions.explanation: If products store more chemical potential energy than reactants, energy must be absorbed during the reaction.

questions.prompt: A student says, "Exothermic means the system gets hotter, so the surroundings do not matter." What is the best correction?
choices: Exothermic reactions are defined by energy released from the system to the surroundings.
Exothermic reactions are defined by energy absorbed from the surroundings.
Exothermic reactions are mainly identified by a larger product mass.
Exothermic reactions depend on the surroundings because energy transfer is involved.
questions.explanation: The key idea is the direction of energy transfer: exothermic reactions release energy from the system to the surroundings.

questions.prompt: What is the main energy transformation in cellular respiration?
choices: Light energy is transformed into chemical potential energy in glucose.
Chemical potential energy in glucose is transformed into ATP and other useful forms.
Nuclear energy is transformed into radiation inside the nucleus.
Sound energy is transformed into carbon dioxide.
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration transforms stored chemical energy in glucose into ATP and other energy forms cells can use.

questions.prompt: Which word equation best matches cellular respiration?
choices: carbon dioxide + water + light energy -> glucose + oxygen
hydrogen + oxygen -> water + electrical current
uranium + neutron -> smaller nuclei + energy
glucose + oxygen -> carbon dioxide + water + energy
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration uses glucose and oxygen and produces carbon dioxide, water, and usable energy.

questions.prompt: Why does cellular respiration not simply burn glucose all at once like a flame?
choices: Cells avoid oxygen when transforming energy from glucose.
The energy is released through controlled steps so cells can capture useful energy in ATP.
Glucose has no chemical potential energy.
Carbon dioxide stores the released energy as light.
questions.explanation: Controlled steps allow cells to transfer some energy into ATP instead of releasing it all rapidly as heat and light.

questions.prompt: What energy transformation happens during photosynthesis?
choices: Chemical potential energy in glucose is transformed into light energy.
Thermal energy from soil is transformed into oxygen gas.
Nuclear energy is transformed into mechanical kinetic energy.
Light energy is transformed into chemical potential energy stored in glucose.
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis stores energy from light in the chemical bonds of glucose.

questions.prompt: Which word equation best summarizes photosynthesis?
choices: glucose + oxygen -> carbon dioxide + water + energy
carbon dioxide + water + light energy -> glucose + oxygen
glucose + oxygen -> carbon dioxide + ATP + water
carbon dioxide + oxygen + thermal energy -> glucose + water
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis uses carbon dioxide, water, and light energy to produce glucose and oxygen.

questions.prompt: What role does chlorophyll play in photosynthesis?
choices: It breaks down glucose during cellular respiration in plant cells.
It absorbs light energy so the energy can be used in reactions that help build glucose.
It stores carbon dioxide as nuclear energy in the chloroplast.
It releases oxygen by reacting directly with fossil fuels.
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll absorbs light energy; that energy helps drive reactions that store chemical potential energy in glucose.

kp-347.title: KP7: Fossil fuels and fuel cells

questions.prompt: Why can fossil fuels be described as storing ancient solar energy?
choices: Ancient organisms stored chemical potential energy originally made through photosynthesis.
Sunlight was trapped in rock layers as radiant energy that stayed unchanged.
Ancient organisms stored gravitational potential energy while buried.
Coal and oil formed when sunlight changed atomic nuclei in sediments.
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis stored solar energy as chemical potential energy in living things, and some remains became fossil fuels over long times.

questions.prompt: Which comparison between burning fossil fuels and using a hydrogen fuel cell is most accurate?
choices: Both transform chemical potential energy and produce carbon dioxide as a main product.
Combustion often releases carbon dioxide and other pollutants, while a hydrogen fuel cell can produce water as its main product.
Fuel cells transform nuclear energy, while fossil fuels transform radiant energy directly.
Fossil fuel combustion transforms electrical energy, while fuel cells transform stored light.
questions.explanation: Fossil fuel combustion commonly releases carbon dioxide and pollutants; hydrogen fuel cells transform chemical energy with water as the main product when operating ideally.

questions.prompt: In a hydrogen fuel cell, why do electrons flowing through an external circuit matter?
choices: They carry hydrogen ions through the membrane to the cathode.
They are an electric current that can power a load such as a motor.
They combine with nitrogen to make fossil fuel.
They show that the fuel cell uses nuclear fission.
questions.explanation: The separated electron flow is electrical energy that can do useful work in a circuit.

questions.prompt: What makes a nuclear reaction different from a chemical reaction?
choices: A chemical reaction changes nuclei, while a nuclear reaction rearranges bonds.
A nuclear reaction changes atomic nuclei, while a chemical reaction rearranges atoms and bonds.
A nuclear reaction changes temperature but not the particles in an atom.
A chemical reaction changes element identity by changing proton numbers.
questions.explanation: Chemical reactions rearrange atoms; nuclear reactions involve changes in the nuclei of atoms.

questions.prompt: Which situation is most likely a nuclear reaction rather than a chemical reaction?
choices: Methane burning in oxygen.
Iron rusting in moist air.
An unstable nucleus changing into a different nucleus while releasing energy.
Sugar dissolving in water.
questions.explanation: A change in the nucleus, especially with energy released, is characteristic of a nuclear reaction.

questions.prompt: A student says, "Nuclear reactions are just very hot chemical reactions." What is the best correction?
choices: Nuclear reactions involve nuclei and can transform much more energy than chemical reactions.
Nuclear reactions are chemical reactions that happen in hotter containers.
Nuclear reactions involve bonds and electrons more than protons or neutrons.
Nuclear reactions are identified mainly by the presence of visible flame.
questions.explanation: The defining difference is the change in atomic nuclei, not simply temperature.

questions.prompt: Two atoms are isotopes of the same element. What must they have in common?
choices: The same number of neutrons.
The same mass number.
The same amount of stored energy.
The same number of protons.
questions.explanation: Isotopes of an element have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.

questions.prompt: Carbon-12 and carbon-14 both have 6 protons. Carbon-14 has 8 neutrons. Why are they isotopes?
choices: They have different numbers of protons but the same mass.
They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
They are different elements because both contain carbon.
They have different electron arrangements but identical nuclei.
questions.explanation: Same proton number means the same element; different neutron number makes them isotopes.

questions.prompt: Why can some isotopes be radioactive?
choices: Their nuclei are unstable and emit particles or radiation to become more stable.
They have chemical bonds that release energy by changing colour.
They contain neutrons and protons in an unstable arrangement.
They become radioactive when electrons move between energy levels.
questions.explanation: Radioactive isotopes have unstable nuclei that release energy or particles as they move toward a more stable state.

questions.prompt: What is nuclear decay?
choices: A chemical reaction where atoms trade electrons in bonds.
A process where molecules freeze into a lower-energy crystal.
A transfer of sound energy from one material to another.
A change to an atom due to emission of particles or radiation from its nucleus.
questions.explanation: Nuclear decay occurs when an unstable nucleus changes by emitting particles or radiation.

questions.prompt: How does radiation transfer energy in this nuclear context?
choices: By moving thermal energy through liquid water pipes.
As electromagnetic waves emitted by atoms or molecules.
By carrying whole atoms from one place to another.
By rearranging chemical bonds without emitting waves.
questions.explanation: Radiation can transfer energy as electromagnetic waves, including high-energy photons in some nuclear processes.

questions.prompt: Which statement best connects radioactive decay to energy release?
choices: An unstable nucleus may emit particles or radiation, carrying energy away from the nucleus.
Radioactive decay means the atom has no nucleus.
Radiation in decay is the same process as thermal conduction through metal.
A radioactive isotope becomes stable by absorbing room-temperature thermal energy.
questions.explanation: Particles or radiation emitted during decay carry energy, helping explain how unstable nuclei release energy.

kp-351.title: KP4: Alpha, beta, and gamma decay

questions.prompt: Which comparison of alpha, beta, and gamma decay is most accurate?
choices: Alpha emits a helium nucleus, beta emits a fast-moving electron, and gamma emits a high-energy photon.
Alpha emits a neutron, beta emits a photon, and gamma emits a helium nucleus.
Alpha and beta change particles in the nucleus, while gamma carries energy as a photon.
Gamma decay changes mass number by 4 and atomic number by 2.
questions.explanation: Alpha, beta, and gamma decay are distinguished by what is emitted: a helium nucleus, an electron, or a high-energy photon.

questions.prompt: An isotope has mass number 210 and atomic number 84. After alpha decay, what are the mass number and atomic number of the new nucleus?
questions.explanation: Alpha decay emits a particle with mass number 4 and atomic number 2, so the new nucleus is 210 - 4 = 206 and 84 - 2 = 82.

questions.prompt: A nucleus gives off a gamma ray after being left in an excited state. What happens to its mass number and atomic number?
choices: Both decrease by 4 and 2.
The atomic number increases by 1, but mass number stays the same.
Neither mass number nor atomic number changes.
The mass number decreases because the photon has no charge.
questions.explanation: Gamma radiation is a photon, so it carries energy away without changing the number of protons or neutrons.

questions.prompt: What happens during nuclear fission?
choices: Two small nuclei combine into a larger nucleus.
A heavy nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
Electrons move to higher energy levels after absorbing light.
Molecules rearrange their chemical bonds without changing nuclei.
questions.explanation: Fission is the splitting of a heavy nucleus into smaller nuclei, with energy released.

questions.prompt: A uranium-235 nucleus absorbs a neutron and becomes unstable. Which outcome best matches a fission event?
choices: It splits into smaller nuclei, releases energy, and may release more neutrons.
It becomes a glucose molecule and releases oxygen.
It forms a heavier stable nucleus without releasing particles.
It absorbs light and releases an electron into a wire.
questions.explanation: In fission, uranium-235 can split into smaller nuclei and release both energy and neutrons.

questions.prompt: Which statement corrects a common misconception about fission?
choices: Fission combines small nuclei, which is why it powers stars.
Fission is not a normal chemical reaction; it involves changes in atomic nuclei.
Fission releases energy because uranium's chemical bonds rearrange.
Fission begins when light energy excites electrons in uranium.
questions.explanation: Fission is nuclear because the nucleus changes; it is not just a rearrangement of chemical bonds.

kp-353.title: KP6: Chain reactions and nuclear reactors

questions.prompt: What makes a fission chain reaction possible?
choices: Neutrons released by one fission event can trigger more fission events nearby.
Light from one atom reflects into another atom and splits it.
Products from photosynthesis immediately split uranium nuclei.
A fission event uses up nearby neutrons before they can affect other nuclei.
questions.explanation: A chain reaction can continue when released neutrons cause additional nuclei to undergo fission.

questions.prompt: Which energy pathway best describes a nuclear power plant?
choices: Nuclear energy -> thermal energy -> mechanical kinetic energy in turbines -> electrical energy.
Light energy -> chemical potential energy -> glucose -> oxygen.
Sound energy -> nuclear energy -> gravitational potential energy.
Electrical energy -> fossil fuel -> carbon dioxide as the main output.
questions.explanation: Fission releases energy as thermal energy, which makes steam move turbines that generate electrical energy.

questions.prompt: Why must a reactor control a fission chain reaction?
choices: To prevent atoms in the coolant from having protons.
To turn uranium directly into sunlight before steam forms.
To make chemical bonds in water disappear during boiling.
To manage the rate of energy release so it can be used safely and steadily.
questions.explanation: A reactor is designed to contain and control fission so the released energy can be transferred and used.

questions.prompt: What happens during nuclear fusion?
choices: A large nucleus splits into two smaller nuclei.
Two very small nuclei combine to form a larger nucleus and release energy.
A molecule dissolves in water and forms ions.
An electron in silicon moves through a wire after absorbing sunlight.
questions.explanation: Fusion combines small nuclei into a larger nucleus and releases energy.

questions.prompt: Why does fusion require extremely high temperatures to begin?
choices: Cold nuclei fuse more easily because they move more slowly.
Fusion starts when molecules freeze into solid crystals.
Small nuclei must collide with enough force to overcome repulsion and fuse.
High temperature changes chemical bonds into nuclear fuel.
questions.explanation: Fusion needs very fast-moving particles so nuclei can collide strongly enough to combine.

questions.prompt: Which comparison between fission and fusion is correct?
choices: Fission splits heavy nuclei; fusion combines small nuclei.
Fission and fusion are both ordinary chemical reactions.
Fusion produces carbon dioxide when hydrogen nuclei combine.
Fission requires chlorophyll, while fusion requires glucose.
questions.explanation: The basic distinction is splitting versus combining nuclei.

questions.prompt: What does absorption mean when light interacts with matter?
choices: Energy is taken up by matter instead of being reflected or transmitted.
Light bounces off a surface without entering the material.
Light passes completely through a material unchanged.
Matter changes light into mass before any energy transfer.
questions.explanation: Absorption occurs when matter takes up energy from incoming light.

questions.prompt: Which observation best suggests that a material is absorbing light energy?
choices: It reflects most incoming light and shows no internal change.
It lets light pass through with little interaction.
It takes in sunlight and becomes warmer or drives a change inside the material.
It remains unchanged while the light travels around it.
questions.explanation: Absorbed light energy can be transformed into thermal energy or used to cause other changes.

questions.prompt: Why is absorption often the first step in light-energy transformations?
choices: The material must take in light energy before that energy can be transformed inside the system.
Reflected light stores chemical potential energy in the reflecting surface.
Transmitted light becomes nuclear energy as it passes through matter.
Light transformations occur in matter that does not take in energy.
questions.explanation: For matter to use light energy, it first needs to absorb that energy.

questions.prompt: What happens to an electron when it absorbs enough light energy?
choices: It changes into a neutron in the nucleus.
It releases its energy before interacting with matter.
It can move to a higher energy level.
It becomes carbon dioxide during photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Absorbed light energy can excite an electron, moving it to a higher energy level.

questions.prompt: Why are excited electrons important in light-energy transformations?
choices: They show that absorbed light was reflected from the material.
They prevent the molecule from changing after light is absorbed.
They change the nucleus into a different element.
Their added energy can be passed into chemical changes, electrical current, or molecular changes.
questions.explanation: Excited electrons provide a way for absorbed light energy to be transformed into other useful forms.

questions.prompt: A student says, "When light is absorbed, the electron disappears." What is the best correction?
choices: The electron changes into a helium nucleus after absorbing light.
The electron gains energy and may move to a higher energy level; it does not simply disappear.
The electron becomes carbon dioxide if the molecule is in a leaf.
The electron reflects the light and stays at a lower energy level.
questions.explanation: Absorption changes the electron's energy state; it does not mean the electron vanishes.

questions.prompt: How does chlorophyll help store energy during photosynthesis?
choices: It breaks down glucose to release ATP before light is absorbed.
It absorbs light energy that can be used to form high-energy chemical bonds.
It stores oxygen as electrical kinetic energy in the chloroplast.
It reflects light energy away so the plant can avoid overheating.
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll absorbs light, and that energy helps drive reactions that store chemical potential energy.

questions.prompt: Which statement best connects chlorophyll to glucose production?
choices: Chlorophyll uses glucose to create light before photosynthesis begins.
Chlorophyll stores nuclear energy in high-energy bonds.
Chlorophyll absorbs light energy that helps power reactions leading to chemical potential energy in glucose.
Chlorophyll keeps electrons at low energy so glucose can form.
questions.explanation: Light absorbed by chlorophyll supports the reaction sequence that produces glucose.

questions.prompt: Which misconception about chlorophyll should be corrected?
choices: Chlorophyll absorbs light energy.
Chlorophyll is found in chloroplasts.
Chlorophyll helps connect light energy to chemical potential energy.
Chlorophyll is just a green colour and has no role in energy transformation.
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll is not just a colour; it is involved in absorbing light energy for photosynthesis.

questions.prompt: What energy transformation happens in a photovoltaic cell?
choices: Chemical potential energy is transformed into glucose.
Nuclear energy is transformed into alpha particles.
Light energy is transformed directly into electrical kinetic energy.
Sound energy is transformed into thermal energy in the silicon.
questions.explanation: Photovoltaic cells use absorbed light to produce moving electrons, which is electrical kinetic energy.

questions.prompt: Why do electrons move in a photovoltaic cell after light is absorbed?
choices: Light energy changes silicon into a chemical fuel.
Light energy frees electrons so they can move through a circuit.
The cell uses sound waves to push electrons through metal.
The electrons become photons after crossing the junction.
questions.explanation: Absorbed light energy can free electrons in the cell, allowing current to flow through conductors.

questions.prompt: Which device most directly uses the same principle as a photovoltaic cell?
choices: A hand warmer releasing thermal energy from a chemical reaction.
A mirror reflecting sunlight onto a wall.
A candle burning wax to produce light.
A solar panel charging a small battery in daylight.
questions.explanation: Solar panels use photovoltaic cells to transform absorbed light into electrical energy.

questions.prompt: What is the role of rhodopsin in vision?
choices: It stores chemical potential energy from food directly in the retina.
It reflects light back out of rod and cone cells.
It absorbs light energy and begins molecular changes that lead to an electrical signal.
It splits nuclei in retinal cells to create a nerve signal.
questions.explanation: Rhodopsin absorbs light in retinal cells and helps start the signal pathway to the brain.

questions.prompt: Which sequence best traces a light-energy transformation in vision?
choices: Light reflects from rhodopsin, then a signal forms without absorption.
Light is absorbed by molecules in retina cells, molecular shape changes occur, and nerve signals are sent.
Light becomes glucose in the retina, then glucose travels through the optic nerve.
Light splits nuclei in rod cells, then neutrons carry the image.
questions.explanation: Vision begins when light is absorbed by molecules in retina cells, causing changes that lead to electrical signals.

questions.prompt: A student says, "Seeing is just light entering the eye; no energy transformation happens." What is the best correction?
choices: Vision begins when light reflects from the retina without interacting with molecules.
Rhodopsin changes glucose directly into sunlight.
A nuclear chain reaction in the retina produces the image.
Absorbed light energy causes molecular changes that help produce electrical signals to the brain.
questions.explanation: Vision involves light absorption and transformation into signals that the nervous system can use.

kp-360.title: KP6: Light-energy transformation in different systems

questions.prompt: Why can absorbed light energy lead to different outcomes in different systems?
choices: The result depends on the material's properties and where the absorbing molecule or material is located.
Absorbed light becomes glucose in any material that takes in light.
Absorbed light becomes electrical current in any living cell.
The result depends mainly on whether the object is heavy.
questions.explanation: Different systems use absorbed light differently because their materials and structures are different.

questions.prompt: Which comparison correctly matches a system to a light-energy transformation?
choices: Chlorophyll: light to nuclear fission; photovoltaic cell: light to glucose; eye: light to coal.
Chlorophyll: reflected light; photovoltaic cell: sound to current; eye: uranium to vision.
The three systems transform light energy into the same final product.
Chlorophyll: light to chemical potential energy; photovoltaic cell: light to electrical energy; eye: light to nerve signals.
questions.explanation: The same starting form, light energy, can be transformed in different ways depending on the system.

questions.prompt: Which design question best applies the idea that light-energy use depends on the system?
choices: How can alpha decay be used to make a green leaf?
What material should absorb light if the goal is to produce current rather than warm a surface?
Why do molecules in different systems give identical outputs after absorbing light?
How can a material reflect light while still using the absorbed energy?
questions.explanation: Choosing the right material matters because absorbed light can be transformed into different useful outputs.

questions.prompt: What makes radiation ionizing?
choices: It warms water by ordinary thermal conduction.
It reflects from shiny surfaces without entering matter.
It can remove electrons from atoms and break chemical bonds.
It changes atoms into sound waves after passing through tissue.
questions.explanation: Ionizing radiation has enough energy to remove electrons from atoms, which can damage molecules and cells.

questions.prompt: Why can ionizing radiation harm living tissue?
choices: It makes cells photosynthesize when they absorb radiation.
It can change molecules by ionizing atoms and breaking bonds needed for normal cell function.
It changes skin colour without affecting cell molecules.
It prevents energy transfer inside tissue, so cells stop warming.
questions.explanation: When bonds in biological molecules are broken or rearranged, cells and tissues may no longer function normally.

questions.prompt: Which example best applies the idea of ionizing radiation?
choices: Visible light passes through clear glass without being absorbed.
A warm spoon transfers thermal energy to cooler water.
A rolling ball transfers mechanical kinetic energy to another ball.
High-energy radiation changes atoms in tissue by removing electrons.
questions.explanation: Ionizing radiation is specifically about radiation energetic enough to ionize atoms, not ordinary heat transfer or motion transfer.

questions.prompt: Why is alpha radiation often less dangerous outside the body than inside the body?
choices: Alpha particles pass deeply through the body before interacting.
Alpha particles deposit energy near the surface they strike, so dead outer skin can block much external damage.
Alpha particles are the same kind of radiation as visible light.
Alpha particles become harmless because they have no charge.
questions.explanation: Alpha particles are massive and charged, so they do not penetrate far but can be very damaging if emitted inside tissue.

questions.prompt: Which situation creates the greatest alpha radiation risk?
choices: An alpha source is outside the body and blocked by outer dead skin cells.
A student hears a sound wave from across the room.
A mirror reflects sunlight onto a wall.
A radioactive alpha emitter is inhaled or swallowed.
questions.explanation: Inside the body, alpha particles can deposit energy directly into living tissue.

questions.prompt: Which property helps explain alpha particles' surface effect?
choices: They have no mass and no charge.
They have relatively high mass and charge compared with beta particles.
They move through tissue like gamma photons.
They are water molecules emitted by nuclei.
