questions.explanation: Measuring air temperature above the surfaces shows how surface energy transfer affects the nearby atmosphere.

questions.prompt: In the equation \(Q = mc\Delta T\), what does \(\Delta T\) represent?
choices: The change in temperature.
The final temperature before heating begins.
The specific heat capacity of the material.
The amount of heat absorbed or released.
questions.explanation: In Q = mc delta T, delta T is the temperature change.

questions.prompt: A 50 g sample with \(c = 2\,J/(g\cdot C)\) warms by 4 C. Using \(Q = mc\Delta T\), how much heat is absorbed?
questions.explanation: Q = 50 g x 2 J/(g C) x 4 C = 400 J.

questions.prompt: A student calculates \(Q\) but forgets to multiply by mass. What kind of error is this?
choices: They use temperature change correctly but leave out how much material is heated.
They are calculating specific heat capacity instead of heat transfer.
They treat mass and specific heat capacity as the same quantity.
They assume the sample's final temperature equals the heat absorbed.
questions.explanation: The equation includes mass because heating more material requires more energy.

kp-418.title: KP4: Sea breezes and land breezes

questions.prompt: Why can a daytime sea breeze form near a coast?
choices: Water warms faster than land, so warm air sinks over the ocean.
Land warms faster than water, warming nearby air so it rises and cooler air moves in from the sea.
Sea breezes form because ocean water has lower specific heat capacity than soil.
The breeze is caused by evaporation without any difference in air temperature.
questions.explanation: Different heating rates between land and water can create pressure and convection patterns that move air.

questions.prompt: At night, land cools faster than nearby water. Which local wind pattern can this help create?
choices: A land breeze moving from land toward the water.
A sea breeze moving from water to land for the same daytime reason.
A breeze caused by land keeping more stored heat than water.
A wind pattern unrelated to different heating and cooling rates.
questions.explanation: At night, warmer air may be over the water, so cooler land air can move seaward.

questions.prompt: Which concept best explains why land and water create different local winds?
choices: Different specific heat capacities cause different heating and cooling rates.
Land and water have the same heat storage but different colours.
Local winds form because water blocks radiation from reaching land.
Specific heat capacity explains sample mass but not temperature change.
questions.explanation: Land and water do not warm and cool at the same rate, and that difference can drive local air movement.

kp-419.title: KP1: Physical models of Earth systems

questions.prompt: Why do scientists use physical models of Earth systems?
choices: Models can represent selected parts of complex systems so variables can be tested.
Models are useful because their results do not need measurements.
Models are strongest when many variables are changed at the same time.
Models work by replacing the real system rather than representing it.
questions.explanation: A model simplifies a complex system so key relationships can be investigated.

questions.prompt: A class builds two small greenhouse-effect models and changes only the covering material. What makes this a useful model test?
choices: The model is useful because the outside temperature is ignored.
The covering material matters because it changes the mass of the thermometer.
One variable is changed while temperature data can be compared.
The test is controlled because the model has no independent variable.
questions.explanation: A controlled model test allows students to connect a design variable to temperature results.

questions.prompt: What is an important limitation of a classroom greenhouse model?
choices: It can show temperature patterns while simplifying the real atmosphere.
It becomes inaccurate mainly because it has measurable variables.
It should match the real atmosphere in size to produce data.
It is limited because classroom materials prevent energy transfer.
questions.explanation: Physical models are useful but simplified, so their limitations should be evaluated.

kp-420.title: KP2: Natural and human-induced greenhouse effects

questions.prompt: Which feature should a greenhouse-effect model include?
choices: Light is reflected before entering, while temperature rises because the system is closed.
The model should prevent energy transfer so temperature changes can be ignored.
Light enters, energy is absorbed, thermal energy forms, and some infrared radiation is limited from leaving.
The model should focus on sound energy because infrared radiation is not involved.
questions.explanation: The model should represent key energy pathways in the greenhouse effect.

questions.prompt: How could a model be adjusted to represent a stronger human-induced greenhouse effect?
choices: Change a variable so more infrared energy is retained, then compare temperature data.
Use the same setup twice but label one trial human-induced.
Remove the outside-temperature comparison to make the model stronger.
Increase the number of thermometers without changing the model conditions.
questions.explanation: The investigation asks students to adjust variables and compare temperature changes.

questions.prompt: Why should the outside temperature also be measured when testing a greenhouse model?
choices: It is the dependent variable, while inside temperature is the controlled variable.
It shows whether the model blocks solar energy before it enters.
It lets students calculate pH changes in the atmosphere model.
It provides a comparison for judging temperature change inside the model.
questions.explanation: Comparing inside and outside temperatures helps evaluate how the model changes energy retention.

questions.prompt: What happens when oceans absorb carbon dioxide from the atmosphere?
choices: Carbon dioxide increases ocean pH by removing acids from seawater.
Carbon dioxide is stored in ocean water without affecting chemical conditions.
Dissolved \(CO_2\) mainly changes seawater temperature rather than acidity.
Some \(CO_2\) reacts with water to form carbonic acid, increasing acidity.
questions.explanation: The investigation explains that absorbed CO2 can react with water to form carbonic acid.

questions.prompt: Why can ocean carbon dioxide absorption be described as both helpful and harmful?
choices: It can moderate atmospheric warming, but increased acidity can harm marine organisms.
It helps by warming the ocean, but harms by lowering atmospheric temperature too much.
It helps marine shells grow by making seawater more acidic.
It is harmful mainly because carbon dioxide changes into table salt.
questions.explanation: Oceans absorb CO2, but more dissolved CO2 can lead to ocean acidification.

questions.prompt: Which equation-style description is most consistent with the checklist idea?
choices: \(CO_2\) + water -> carbonic acid, leading to increased ocean acidity.
\(CO_2\) + water -> oxygen gas, leading to less acidity.
\(CO_2\) + shell material -> thermal energy, leading to warmer eggshells.
\(CO_2\) + sunlight -> calcium carbonate, leading to stronger shells.
questions.explanation: The key relationship is that carbon dioxide can react with water to form carbonic acid.

questions.prompt: Why can increased ocean acidity harm animals such as clams, corals, and sea urchins?
choices: It helps shells form by turning acid into extra calcium carbonate.
It can make it harder for them to build calcium-containing shells or skeletons.
It mainly affects shell colour while leaving shell strength unchanged.
It affects shelled organisms by changing sound energy in seawater.
questions.explanation: The textbook connects acidic conditions to difficulty building shells and skeletons.

questions.prompt: In an eggshell model of ocean acidity, what does the eggshell represent?
choices: A greenhouse gas molecule dissolved in the ocean.
A calcium-containing shell or skeleton of a marine organism.
A model of ocean currents moving heat around Earth.
A container that measures the pH of seawater directly.
questions.explanation: Eggshells can model calcium-containing structures affected by acidic water.

questions.prompt: A shell sample loses mass after sitting in a more acidic liquid. What conclusion best fits the model?
choices: Greater acidity can damage calcium-containing shell material.
The shell gained mass but the scale measured acidity instead.
A mass decrease shows the shell was protected from chemical change.
The liquid's pH changed because the shell became more like a greenhouse gas.
questions.explanation: A mass decrease or visible shell damage can model how acidity affects calcium shells.

questions.prompt: Why should students evaluate both strengths and limitations of an Earth-system model?
choices: A model's value depends on hiding its assumptions from the investigation.
A model is strongest when its limits are treated as extra evidence.
Models simplify real systems, so they can show patterns while leaving out details.
Models are useful because they avoid comparisons with real Earth systems.
questions.explanation: Models are useful simplifications, but students should identify what they represent well and what they miss.

questions.prompt: Which is a limitation of using eggshells to model ocean acidification?
choices: Eggshells are not living marine organisms in a complete ocean ecosystem.
Eggshells represent acidity but not calcium-containing material.
Eggshells are useful when their mass measurement is left out.
Eggshells make the model stronger by removing chemical reactions.
questions.explanation: Eggshells can model shell material, but they do not include the full biology and ecology of real organisms.

questions.prompt: Which model result would be useful evidence but still need careful interpretation?
choices: Shell mass changes in liquids with different acidity levels.
A drawing of the setup made before any test was run.
A class vote about which liquid looks most like seawater.
A result from one container with no comparison liquid.
questions.explanation: Measured shell changes can support conclusions, but the model's limits should still be considered.

kp-424.title: KP1: Human reliance on energy transformations

questions.prompt: What is the main message of Chapter 3.4 Concept 1?
choices: Energy technologies are best evaluated by benefits without considering impacts.
Human societies use energy mainly in ways unrelated to transformation.
Energy transformation risks are limited to natural events such as volcanoes.
Energy transformations provide benefits for humans but can also create risks or harm.
questions.explanation: The chapter frames human energy use as a balance of benefits and possible negative effects.

questions.prompt: Which evaluation question best fits a new energy technology?
choices: What benefits does it provide, and what environmental or social harms might it cause?
Does it produce useful energy while making trade-offs easier to ignore?
Can its risks be judged without considering who is affected?
Does it use a familiar energy source, regardless of its impacts?
questions.explanation: Responsible evaluation considers both usefulness and risks.

questions.prompt: A technology provides cheap electricity but damages habitat. What does this example show?
choices: Energy technologies can involve trade-offs between benefits and harms.
Low cost is enough evidence that the technology has a positive impact.
Habitat damage should be evaluated separately from energy decisions.
Environmental harm occurs mainly when energy is not transformed usefully.
questions.explanation: Many energy decisions involve trade-offs that must be weighed carefully.

questions.prompt: How does photosynthesis support human survival in energy terms?
choices: Plants release chemical energy by carrying out cellular respiration for humans.
Plants store thermal energy from the Sun directly as body movement.
Plants transform solar energy into chemical potential energy in food.
Plants transform sunlight into matter without storing chemical energy.
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis stores energy in chemical form, which supports food webs including humans.

questions.prompt: What does cellular respiration do with chemical potential energy from food?
choices: It stores solar energy in food before plants can use it.
It transforms stored chemical energy into forms cells can use for life functions.
It converts food energy into gravitational potential energy for ecosystems.
It is the process that builds food molecules from carbon dioxide and water.
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration releases usable energy from food molecules.

questions.prompt: Which sequence best connects solar energy to human movement?
choices: Sunlight -> body movement -> food energy -> cellular respiration -> photosynthesis.
Sunlight -> chemical energy in food without organisms -> movement without respiration.
Sunlight -> thermal energy in air -> shell formation -> human movement.
Sunlight -> photosynthesis in plants -> chemical energy in food -> cellular respiration -> movement.
questions.explanation: Human activity ultimately depends on energy transformations in living systems.

kp-426.title: KP3: Fire and early human technology

questions.prompt: What energy transformation occurs when wood burns in a fire?
choices: Thermal energy in the air is stored as chemical potential energy in wood.
Chemical potential energy transforms into thermal and radiant energy.
Radiant energy from flames changes directly into gravitational potential energy.
Mechanical energy in wood changes into electrical energy for cooking.
questions.explanation: Burning wood releases stored chemical energy as heat and light.

questions.prompt: Which benefit of fire-starting technology is most directly linked to thermal energy?
choices: Cooking food and staying warm in colder climates.
Storing sunlight in plant tissue through photosynthesis.
Producing electrical energy without burning fuel.
Reducing the need for radiant energy at night by removing light.
questions.explanation: Thermal energy from fire helped people cook and survive in cold environments.

questions.prompt: Why is fire a good example of technology based on energy transformation?
choices: It shows how a useful technology can control the release of stored energy.
It works because matter changes while energy stays separate from the process.
It is useful mainly because wood stores thermal energy before it burns.
It changes radiant energy into chemical potential energy for later use.
questions.explanation: Fire-starting let humans control a useful energy transformation.

kp-427.title: KP4: Large-scale combustion and air pollution

questions.prompt: What energy transformation powered many steam engines?
choices: Chemical potential energy in coal -> thermal energy -> mechanical kinetic energy.
Thermal energy in steam -> chemical potential energy in coal -> motion.
Mechanical kinetic energy in wheels -> coal formation -> electrical energy.
Radiant energy from the Sun -> ocean acidity -> vehicle motion.
questions.explanation: Coal combustion produced heat, which was used to create mechanical motion in steam engines.

questions.prompt: What harmful effect can large-scale combustion produce?
choices: Cleaner air when smoke mixes with fog.
Air pollution and health problems.
A lower risk of smog because combustion uses oxygen.
Less pollution if the fuel is burned at a larger scale.
questions.explanation: The textbook connects coal smoke, smog, and vehicle emissions to air pollution and health risks.

questions.prompt: Why is a gas engine an example of both benefit and harm?
choices: It can provide transportation but also contribute to smog and air pollution.
It is beneficial because combustion products stay inside the engine.
It is harmful mainly because it converts motion back into unused fuel.
It avoids trade-offs because mechanical motion is a useful output.
questions.explanation: Combustion engines helped transportation but also brought pollution-related problems.

questions.prompt: Why is cement production included as an energy-transformation trade-off?
choices: It provides useful building material but can release significant carbon dioxide.
It reduces climate impact because chemical reactions absorb carbon dioxide during production.
It is mainly a trade-off because cement stores little usable thermal energy.
It avoids environmental harm because it is not an electrical technology.
questions.explanation: Cement is useful, but its production can add greenhouse gas emissions.

questions.prompt: What energy transformation occurs in a battery when it powers a device?
choices: Electrical energy is stored by changing it into gravitational potential energy.
Chemical potential energy transforms into electrical energy.
Thermal energy from the device changes into unused cement.
Radiant energy from the screen changes into battery chemicals.
questions.explanation: Batteries store chemical potential energy that can be transformed into electrical energy.

questions.prompt: What hidden harm can batteries create if they are not recycled properly?
choices: They can reduce landfill problems by releasing their metals into soil.
They become safer when mixed with other waste because concentration decreases.
Toxic chemicals can leach into landfills and harm health or ecosystems.
They create harm mainly by preventing devices from using electricity.
questions.explanation: The textbook notes that modern batteries can create toxic waste problems if not handled properly.

questions.prompt: What energy transformation is central to hydroelectric dams?
choices: Gravitational potential and kinetic energy of water are transformed into electrical energy.
Electrical energy in turbines is transformed into stored water behind the dam.
Thermal energy in decaying plants is transformed directly into moving water.
Chemical potential energy in concrete is transformed into electrical energy.
questions.explanation: Falling or flowing water drives turbines and generators to produce electricity.

questions.prompt: Which is a possible harm of building a large hydroelectric reservoir?
choices: Reducing habitat impact because water covers affected land evenly.
Creating electricity while avoiding social impacts on nearby communities.
Making methane less relevant because the energy source is renewable.
Flooding land, releasing methane from decaying plants, or displacing people.
questions.explanation: Hydroelectricity provides useful power, but dam construction can flood land and affect communities and ecosystems.

questions.prompt: Which statement best evaluates hydroelectric power fairly?
choices: It can provide electrical energy, but its environmental and social impacts must be considered.
It should be judged by electricity output without considering flooded land.
It is harmful mainly because it does not involve energy transformation.
It avoids trade-offs because water is part of a natural cycle.
questions.explanation: A balanced evaluation includes both the energy benefit and possible harms.

kp-430.title: KP7: Nuclear reactors and fuel cells

questions.prompt: How can a nuclear reactor produce electrical energy?
choices: Nuclear energy is transformed into thermal energy, steam motion, and then electrical energy.
Nuclear energy changes into electricity without heating water or moving a generator.
Thermal energy from steam is transformed back into nuclear fuel.
Electrical energy from the grid is transformed into medical isotopes.
questions.explanation: Reactors use nuclear energy to produce heat, which can make steam that drives generators.

questions.prompt: Why is nuclear technology considered both useful and risky?
choices: It can generate electricity and medical isotopes, but radiation can harm living things.
It is useful mainly because radiation risk disappears inside reactors.
It is risky because nuclear energy is too indirect to produce electrical energy.
It is beneficial for medicine but unrelated to energy transformations.
questions.explanation: The textbook notes benefits from reactors and isotopes while emphasizing radiation risk.

questions.prompt: What hidden impact can hydrogen fuel cells have?
choices: Fuel cells may be emission-free during use, while hydrogen production can still release carbon dioxide.
Hydrogen production matters less because fuel cells produce no useful energy.
Fuel cells are hidden impacts mainly because they use nuclear waste.
Hydrogen fuel avoids trade-offs because it is the lightest element.
questions.explanation: Fuel cells may emit no pollutants during use, but hydrogen production can still have emissions.

questions.prompt: What kind of action can reduce harmful impacts of technology-related energy transformations?
choices: Both personal choices and national or collective decisions.
Private choices matter, while infrastructure and policy are separate from energy impacts.
National decisions matter, while daily energy choices do not contribute.
Action is useful mainly after a technology has no environmental effects.
questions.explanation: The chapter describes both individual and collective action as important for reducing harm.

questions.prompt: Which action best matches the concept of reducing energy-related harm?
choices: Replacing one energy technology with another without evaluating impacts.
Using more fuel now because future technology may reduce harm later.
Choosing sustainable technologies and reducing unnecessary energy use.
Reducing visible pollution while ignoring waste from the technology.
questions.explanation: Reducing energy use and choosing better technologies can lessen harmful impacts.

questions.prompt: Why are national actions often needed in addition to personal actions?
choices: Some energy systems and technologies are too large for individuals to change alone.
Personal actions reduce harm mainly when national systems are not involved.
National action is mainly symbolic because energy infrastructure is local.
Large energy systems respond to consumer habits but not policy choices.
questions.explanation: Large-scale energy choices often require policy, infrastructure, and collective planning.

questions.prompt: Why can reducing energy-related harm require trade-offs?
choices: It may require commitment and sacrifices in comfort or convenience.
It works best when benefits are considered but sacrifices are ignored.
It means choosing between comfort and environment with no middle ground.
It requires new technologies because personal choices have no role.
questions.explanation: The textbook notes that action can require sacrifices, even when the goal is worthwhile.

questions.prompt: Which example best shows a trade-off in reducing harm?
choices: Cycling instead of driving reduces combustion pollution but may take more effort or time.
Driving farther to reduce fuel use because the trip feels more efficient.
Recycling batteries by placing them with regular landfill waste.
Choosing convenience while assuming pollution changes automatically.
questions.explanation: Reducing emissions can involve giving up some convenience.

questions.prompt: What misconception about sustainability choices should be corrected?
choices: That benefits and sacrifices can both be evaluated.
That comfort can be part of decision-making.
That energy decisions can affect other people.
That a useful action loses value if it requires some sacrifice.
questions.explanation: Responsible decisions often involve weighing benefits, costs, and sacrifices.

questions.prompt: How can an urban farm program reduce harmful energy impacts?
choices: It reduces harm mainly by replacing photosynthesis with electricity.
It changes food production so transportation energy is no longer measurable.
It increases local energy use but removes the need to evaluate transport.
It can reduce pollution from transporting food over long distances.
questions.explanation: Local food initiatives can reduce transportation-related energy use and pollution.

questions.prompt: Why can recycling programs reduce harmful impacts of energy transformation?
choices: They can reduce landfill waste and the need for manufacturing new materials.
They work mainly by moving manufacturing impacts to another community.
They reduce harm because recycled materials no longer require transport.
They are helpful when recycling uses more new raw material.
questions.explanation: Recycling can reduce the impacts of manufacturing and disposal.

questions.prompt: Which student action most directly reduces combustion-related pollution?
choices: Cycling to school instead of travelling by car when practical.
Leaving a car idling because the trip distance is shorter.
Recycling materials while increasing vehicle trips for the same route.
Choosing local food but transporting it farther before use.
questions.explanation: Cycling can reduce fuel combustion and related air pollution.

questions.prompt: Why does the textbook warn that technology is rarely a cure-all?
choices: New technologies can reduce one harm while creating other impacts.
A new technology should be judged by its label rather than its full life cycle.
A technology that reduces emissions during use has solved the whole problem.
Technology is separate from social choices about energy use.
questions.explanation: Technologies should be evaluated across their full life cycle, not just their immediate benefits.

questions.prompt: Which evaluation of electric vehicles is most balanced?
choices: They reduce tailpipe emissions, while electricity generation and battery disposal still matter.
They solve transportation pollution because the vehicle itself has no exhaust pipe.
They have hidden impacts mainly if the driver uses them for long trips.
They should be evaluated by battery disposal but not by electricity source.
questions.explanation: Electric vehicles can reduce some emissions, but upstream energy sources and battery waste must be considered.

questions.prompt: What is a common misconception about emerging technologies?
choices: That technologies can be compared by looking at benefits and harms.
That batteries can contain materials that need careful disposal.
That electricity sources can affect the impact of an electric vehicle.
That a technology labelled clean has no hidden impacts.
questions.explanation: A technology may reduce visible pollution while still having hidden production, disposal, or energy-source impacts.

questions.prompt: Why is controlled nuclear fusion difficult to use for electricity generation?
choices: It requires extremely high temperatures that must be sustained and contained.
It is difficult because the reaction needs to be cooled to near freezing.
It works best when the plasma transfers heat directly through the reactor walls.
It is difficult mainly because fusion produces no thermal energy.
questions.explanation: Fusion is promising, but sustaining and containing super-heated reactions is a major challenge.

questions.prompt: What is the role of magnetic fields in a tokamak reactor?
choices: They cool the reaction to freezing temperatures.
They help contain the super-heated fusion reaction away from reactor walls.
They turn fusion energy directly into stored chemical energy.
They increase friction between plasma and reactor walls.
questions.explanation: Magnetic confinement keeps the extremely hot plasma from touching material walls.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains why fusion is still an emerging technology?
choices: Scientists can create extreme conditions, but reliable long-term containment remains hard.
Fusion has been delayed because it releases too little energy in a reactor.
Fusion is mainly limited by the lack of magnetic field technology in classrooms.
Fusion is already ready for ordinary power grids once fuel cells improve.
questions.explanation: The difficulty is not only starting fusion, but sustaining and controlling it for useful electricity.

questions.prompt: Why is hydrogen production important when evaluating hydrogen fuel cells?
choices: Fuel cells may emit no pollutants during use, but producing hydrogen can release carbon dioxide.
Hydrogen production matters mainly for cost, not for climate impact.
The use stage is enough to judge impact because the fuel is already made.
Fuel cells are lower impact mainly because hydrogen is produced from methane.
questions.explanation: The full impact depends on how the hydrogen fuel is produced.

questions.prompt: How does methane cracking compare with steam methane reforming in the textbook?
choices: Methane cracking can produce hydrogen with much lower carbon dioxide emissions.
Methane cracking reduces tailpipe emissions but raises hydrogen-production emissions.
Steam methane reforming is lower impact because it releases carbon dioxide slowly.
Both methods should be judged by fuel-cell emissions during driving.
questions.explanation: The chapter presents methane cracking as a lower-emission approach to hydrogen production.

questions.prompt: Which question best evaluates whether a hydrogen vehicle is truly lower impact?
choices: Does the vehicle release pollutants while it is being driven?
How was the hydrogen produced, and what emissions came from that process?
Does the vehicle use hydrogen rather than a battery?
Is hydrogen production hidden from the driver?
questions.explanation: Use-stage emissions are not the whole story; fuel production matters too.

questions.prompt: Why is cement production an important target for reducing greenhouse gas emissions?
choices: Cement is produced in huge quantities and its chemical production can release carbon dioxide.
Cement matters mainly because it is transported by vehicles, not because of production.
Cement has low climate impact because it is used in buildings for a long time.
Cement production is less important when each project uses a small amount.
questions.explanation: Because cement is widely used, even reducing emissions per unit can matter greatly.

questions.prompt: A new cement process reduces emissions by 70 percent. If an old process released 100 units of CO2, about how many units would the new process release?
choices: 70 units.
30 units.
100 units.
170 units.
questions.explanation: A 70 percent reduction leaves 30 percent of the original emissions: 30 units.

questions.prompt: Which new cement idea could reduce net carbon dioxide impact?
choices: Cement that releases carbon dioxide earlier in the building process.
Cement that reduces emissions during production but requires more total cement.
Cement that absorbs carbon dioxide as it hardens.
Cement that shifts emissions from production to disposal without reducing them.
questions.explanation: The textbook mentions research into cement that absorbs CO2 during hardening.

questions.prompt: How can societies reduce harm from energy technologies after a failure?
choices: Use new knowledge and changed perspectives to improve future decisions.
Treat the failure as unusual, so the technology design needs little review.
Focus on cleanup while leaving future design decisions unchanged.
Use the same system again if the original benefits were important.
questions.explanation: The concept emphasizes learning from mistakes and applying new understanding.

questions.prompt: Which action best shows learning from a past energy-related harm?
choices: Inspecting systems after an accident and redesigning weak parts.
Making communication faster while leaving safety systems unchanged.
Studying the event mainly to assign blame rather than reduce risk.
Keeping the old design because the accident has already happened.
questions.explanation: Using accident evidence to improve safety is a key way to reduce future harm.

questions.prompt: What does a changed perspective help people do?
choices: Look back at decisions and identify what should be done differently.
Explain past harm as unavoidable so future plans can stay the same.
Focus on technical facts while setting aside community experience.
Treat new evidence as less useful than the original plan.
questions.explanation: New perspectives can reveal risks or harms that were not considered before.

questions.prompt: Which chain of events best matches the Fukushima Daiichi accident summary?
choices: Earthquake and tsunami -> flooding and power loss -> cooling failure -> meltdowns and radioactive release.
Power loss -> more cooling -> lower reactor temperature -> no radioactive release.
Tsunami -> stronger backup generators -> safer cooling -> routine shutdown.
Earthquake -> fuel-cell reaction -> hydrogen production -> lower radiation risk.
questions.explanation: The accident involved natural hazards disabling power and cooling systems, leading to reactor damage and releases.

questions.prompt: Why did disabled backup generators matter during the Fukushima accident?
choices: They mattered because backup power mainly controls public communication.
They were important because cooling depends on lighting rather than electricity.
They were needed to run cooling systems after the power outage.
They increased risk by cooling the reactor cores too quickly.
questions.explanation: Without emergency power, cooling mechanisms could not operate properly.

questions.prompt: What systems lesson can be drawn from the accident?
choices: Multiple failures can interact and make an energy disaster worse.
A natural hazard is separate from energy technology once a plant is built.
Backup systems matter less when the main system is carefully designed.
A system failure is easier to manage when several parts fail together.
questions.explanation: The accident shows how connected system failures can multiply risk.

questions.prompt: Why can cleanup after a nuclear accident be dangerous?
choices: People and environments may be exposed to harmful radioactive materials.
Cleanup is safer when workers enter quickly before mapping contamination.
Radiation exposure is mainly a public-relations issue after the accident ends.
Radioactive materials are less important once they leave the reactor building.
questions.explanation: The textbook notes that radioactive materials escaped and cleanup workers were exposed to radiation.

questions.prompt: Which safety priority best follows from radiation risk?
choices: Focus on visible damage because invisible radiation is harder to measure.
Move contaminated material quickly before deciding where it should go.
Reduce public concern by releasing less information about risk.
Limit exposure, monitor contamination, and plan cleanup carefully.
questions.explanation: Radiation risk requires protective planning and careful management of contaminated areas.

questions.prompt: A cleanup team measures radiation before entering an area. What is the purpose of this step?
choices: To decide whether protective equipment and exposure limits are needed.
To make radiation levels lower by observing them carefully.
To measure public trust instead of environmental contamination.
To show that cleanup is complete before workers begin.
questions.explanation: Measurement helps guide safe decisions for workers and nearby communities.

kp-441.title: KP4: Safety inspections and technology redesign

questions.prompt: What is one way governments and engineers can reduce future risk after an energy accident?
choices: Inspect existing systems and redesign weak technologies.
Focus on public reassurance before checking technical systems.
Keep older systems unchanged if the failure was triggered by nature.
Improve efficiency first and review safety after expansion.
questions.explanation: The Fukushima example shows how inspections and design improvements can follow an accident.

questions.prompt: Why was reassessing older reactor designs important after Fukushima?
choices: Older designs may have safety weaknesses that become clear after a major failure.
Older designs should be kept because their risks are already familiar.
A design flaw matters mainly when the reactor is new.
Reassessment is useful mainly if the accident happened in the same country.
questions.explanation: Learning from failure can reveal design flaws that need to be corrected.

questions.prompt: Which redesign goal best follows from a cooling-system failure?
choices: Make emergency cooling more reliable during power loss or flooding.
Place backup systems where the same hazard can disable them together.
Reduce monitoring so operators are not distracted during emergencies.
Keep cooling systems dependent on the original power source.
questions.explanation: A safer design would reduce the chance that one event disables critical cooling.

questions.prompt: Why might different countries respond differently to the same nuclear accident?
choices: They may weigh safety, reliability, climate impact, costs, and public trust differently.
A technical accident leads to the same policy choice in each country.
Countries with similar reactors will have matching public attitudes.
Public trust matters less when the energy source is dependable.
questions.explanation: Energy policy depends on technical evidence, values, risk tolerance, and public trust.

questions.prompt: Which response is an example of turning away from nuclear power after an accident?
choices: Reducing nuclear power use because public trust and safety concerns changed.
Continuing the same nuclear plan while improving inspection procedures.
Expanding nuclear power because the accident happened elsewhere.
Treating waste concerns as separate from nuclear policy.
questions.explanation: Some nations may decide the risks and trust issues outweigh the benefits.

questions.prompt: Which response is an example of continuing nuclear power while learning from an accident?
choices: Reducing nuclear power because public trust is difficult to rebuild quickly.
Developing safer technology and improving inspections.
Keeping the same design while adding more public debate.
Continuing nuclear power while treating waste as a future issue.
questions.explanation: Some countries continue using nuclear energy while trying to reduce future risk.

questions.prompt: Why must used nuclear fuel be carefully managed?
choices: It can remain hazardous and must be stored so it does not harm life or land.
It becomes a short-term issue once the electricity has been generated.
It is mainly a storage issue because it takes up space, not because of radiation.
It is easier to manage when placed where people have less information.
questions.explanation: Used nuclear fuel requires long-term management because radioactive materials can harm living things and environments.

questions.prompt: Which question should be included in a nuclear energy decision?
choices: How will used fuel be isolated and monitored over time?
How can waste planning be delayed until after the plant closes?
Which storage site is most convenient for industry regardless of local concerns?
How quickly can used fuel be moved before its long-term risk is studied?
questions.explanation: Long-term waste management is part of evaluating nuclear energy.

questions.prompt: Why is nuclear waste management a community issue, not only a technical issue?
choices: Storage locations can affect local land, people, and future generations.
Community questions matter mainly after engineers choose the site.
Technical containment removes the need to consider land relationships.
Waste decisions are local during construction, but less important over time.
questions.explanation: Decisions about storage involve safety, land, trust, and long-term responsibility.

kp-444.title: KP7: Indigenous knowledge and Western science

questions.prompt: Why should nuclear waste planning include Indigenous knowledge and Western science?
choices: Both can contribute evidence, values, and place-based understanding for safer decisions.
Western science can answer technical questions, while local knowledge is unrelated to storage.
Indigenous knowledge matters mainly for communication after a decision is made.
Combining knowledge systems is useful mainly when communities already agree.
questions.explanation: The textbook describes collaboration that includes Indigenous perspectives in storage-site decisions.

questions.prompt: Which group should be meaningfully consulted when a storage site may affect their territory?
choices: Local communities and First Nations, Inuit, and Metis representatives.
Distant experts first, with local voices added after site approval.
Communities that support the plan, while concerns are handled later.
People near transport routes, but not people near the storage location.
questions.explanation: Nuclear waste decisions can affect local lands and communities, so those voices matter.

questions.prompt: What misconception should be avoided in nuclear waste planning?
choices: That evidence is important for storage-site decisions.
That local knowledge can help identify concerns.
That technical safety alone settles social, cultural, and ethical concerns.
That long-term storage requires careful planning.
questions.explanation: Storage decisions involve science, safety, community trust, land relationships, and long-term responsibility.

questions.prompt: Which two concerns strongly influence public opinion against nuclear power?
choices: Accident risk and nuclear waste risk.
Electricity reliability and lower climate emissions.
Fuel-cell emissions and cement production.
Urban farming and recycling programs.
questions.explanation: The nuclear power debate often focuses on accident danger and waste management.

questions.prompt: Why can nuclear waste be a major argument against nuclear power?
choices: It is a concern mainly because it is expensive to transport.
It is less important if the reactor produces dependable electricity.
It requires safe long-term storage and can remain hazardous.
It affects public opinion but not long-term energy planning.
questions.explanation: Waste risk is a central concern because radioactive materials can persist.

questions.prompt: Why can accident risk affect public opinion even if accidents are rare?
choices: A rare accident can have serious effects on people, ecosystems, and trust.
A rare event is usually ignored when the energy source is dependable.
Accidents affect public opinion mainly when electricity costs increase.
Accident risk matters less when a country needs energy independence.
questions.explanation: Low probability does not mean low consequence, so public concern can remain high.

questions.prompt: Which is a common argument in favour of nuclear power?
choices: It can provide dependable electrical energy with lower climate emissions than fossil fuels.
It is dependable mainly because accident planning is less important than output.
It reduces climate impact by producing no waste that requires management.
It is favoured because it avoids public concerns about safety and trust.
questions.explanation: Supporters often point to reliability, lower greenhouse gas emissions, and energy security.

questions.prompt: How can nuclear power reduce dependence on energy imports?
choices: It reduces imports because nuclear waste can be stored outside the country.
It affects imports mainly by replacing electricity with medical isotopes.
A country with nuclear generation may produce more of its own electricity domestically.
It makes energy imports less visible while keeping the same supply source.
questions.explanation: Domestic electricity generation can reduce reliance on imported energy sources.

questions.prompt: Which statement is the most balanced pro-nuclear argument?
choices: Nuclear power has risks, but it can be dependable and contribute less to climate change than fossil fuels.
Nuclear power is best supported by discussing reliability while setting waste aside.
Nuclear power is lower impact because public trust is a social issue, not an energy issue.
Nuclear power should be favoured mainly because renewable sources vary with weather.
questions.explanation: A strong argument acknowledges risks while explaining benefits.

questions.prompt: How did Fukushima affect public trust in nuclear power?
choices: It raised concerns about accident management, communication, and institutional trust.
It mainly changed public opinion by proving nuclear power is less dependable than solar.
It affected trust mainly in countries that already opposed nuclear power.
It showed that technical reliability matters while communication has little influence.
questions.explanation: The textbook notes concerns about slow or contradictory information and trust in accident management.

questions.prompt: Why does communication during an energy disaster matter?
choices: Communication is useful mainly after technical experts finish making decisions.
Slow information can help avoid confusion by reducing public attention.
Contradictory information matters less if the energy source is dependable.
Clear and timely information helps people judge risk and trust response decisions.
questions.explanation: Public confidence depends partly on reliable, transparent communication.

questions.prompt: Which factor can shape nuclear policy in addition to technical evidence?
choices: Public confidence and cultural attitudes toward risk.
The size of a report, because longer reports settle trust questions.
The age of a reactor, because older technology tends to gain trust.
The number of countries using nuclear power, regardless of public concerns.
questions.explanation: Energy decisions involve evidence, emotions, culture, and trust.

questions.prompt: Which set of factors should be weighed in an evidence-based position on nuclear power?
choices: Reliability and cost, while safety and waste are public-opinion issues.
Safety, cost, waste, reliability, and climate change.
Climate change benefits, with accident risks handled after a decision.
Personal concern first, then evidence that supports that concern.
questions.explanation: The textbook debate prompt asks students to consider multiple evidence-based factors.

questions.prompt: Which claim would need stronger evidence before being used in a debate?
choices: Nuclear power can produce electricity.
Nuclear waste must be managed.
Nuclear power is perfect and has no costs or risks.
Safety is relevant to public opinion.
questions.explanation: Extreme claims require strong evidence and should be checked for bias or missing information.

questions.prompt: What makes an argument about nuclear power stronger?
choices: Using evidence and acknowledging trade-offs.
Selecting evidence from one side so the argument is easier to follow.
Using emotional language to replace uncertain data.
Discussing climate benefits without comparing them to safety and waste concerns.
questions.explanation: A reasoned argument weighs evidence from multiple sides.

questions.prompt: What is the central question of the energy transformation debate?
choices: Do technology-based energy transformations benefit society more than they harm it?
Which technology sounds most advanced when described in a debate?
Can one benefit prove that an energy transformation is worth using?
Should harms be evaluated after a technology becomes common?
questions.explanation: The investigation asks students to debate the balance of benefit and harm.

questions.prompt: Which topic would fit the debate investigation?
choices: A description of how a technology works without considering impacts.
A debate about which energy source is popular with no evidence.
Benefits and drawbacks of energy transformations in transportation or electricity generation.
A topic that lists benefits but treats harms as background details.
questions.explanation: The investigation suggests topics such as transportation, electricity, agriculture, manufacturing, medicine, and food systems.

questions.prompt: Why is this debate not answered by a simple yes or no?
choices: Different technologies have different benefits, harms, evidence, and stakeholder impacts.
The answer depends mainly on which side speaks first.
The debate is difficult because benefits are scientific and harms are opinions.
Evidence is less useful when several stakeholders are affected.
questions.explanation: The balance of benefit and harm depends on the technology and evidence considered.

questions.prompt: What should a debate research plan include?
choices: A conclusion first, with sources chosen later to match it.
A list of technologies without deciding what evidence is needed.
Roles for group members, but no plan for checking source reliability.
Clear questions, reliable resources, roles, organized findings, and citations.
questions.explanation: The investigation asks teams to plan questions, resources, roles, records, and citations.

questions.prompt: Why should sources be cited during an energy transformation debate?
choices: So evidence can be checked and claims can be traced to reliable information.
So the speaker can use fewer facts while appearing organized.
So the audience knows which side the team supports before hearing evidence.
So emotional claims can be presented as if they were measured data.
questions.explanation: Citations allow others to evaluate the quality and reliability of evidence.

questions.prompt: Which research question is strongest for a debate about electric vehicles?
choices: How do electric vehicles compare with gasoline vehicles in emissions, battery waste, cost, and reliability?
Are electric vehicles good if the team already supports them?
Which electric vehicle feature sounds most futuristic?
Can electricity generation be left out if the vehicle has no exhaust pipe?
questions.explanation: A strong research question points to evidence that can compare benefits and harms.

questions.prompt: Which question helps identify possible bias in a source about energy technology?
choices: Does the source include a clear opinion that agrees with my side?
Who might benefit from this message, and who paid for it?
Is the source easy to read and visually polished?
Does the source mention a technology that sounds familiar?
questions.explanation: The investigation asks students to consider special interests, funding, and who stands to gain.

questions.prompt: Which source feature most improves reliability for an energy debate?
choices: Facts and statistics are cited so readers can check the evidence.
The source uses confident wording even when evidence is not cited.
The source presents one side clearly and leaves out complications.
The source avoids statistics so the argument feels easier to understand.
questions.explanation: Citations and checkable evidence help a reader evaluate reliability.

questions.prompt: A website about fuel cells is funded by a company that sells hydrogen equipment. What should a student do?
choices: Use the information cautiously and compare it with independent sources.
Reject the source before checking whether its evidence is cited.
Use the source as the main evidence because industry knows the product well.
Treat funding as a minor detail if the source includes statistics.
questions.explanation: Funding does not automatically make a source useless, but it is a reason to check for bias and compare evidence.

questions.prompt: What makes communication in an energy transformation debate evidence-based?
choices: It uses facts, logic, citations, and clear reasoning.
It uses confident opinions and adds citations after the conclusion.
It focuses on the strongest benefit while leaving harms for the other side.
It relies on emotional examples because technical evidence is harder to explain.
questions.explanation: Strong scientific communication supports claims with reliable evidence and reasoning.

questions.prompt: Which statement would be strongest in a debate about technology-based energy transformations?
choices: This technology has these benefits and harms, supported by these cited data.
This technology should be accepted because its benefits are easier to explain.
The opposing view has concerns, but my side has a stronger slogan.
The technology is useful, so detailed evidence about harms can come later.
questions.explanation: Balanced, cited evidence is stronger than unsupported opinion.

questions.prompt: After hearing both sides of a debate, how should the better-supported side be evaluated?
choices: By which side presented fewer uncertainties.
By the quality, reliability, balance, and logic of its evidence.
By which side made its conclusion sound simpler.
By which side used the most examples from one viewpoint.
questions.explanation: Evaluation should focus on evidence quality and reasoning, not presentation tricks alone.
